courseMetadata.title: Adulting for Real — A Quality-of-Life Course
courseMetadata.description: A practical course for young adults (roughly ages 22–30) covering the highest-leverage life skills that are rarely taught directly: meta-skills for starting and sticking with things, the physical base layer of sleep, cooking, and strength, money foundations, credit and consumer self-defense, growing and protecting wealth, communication and negotiation, navigating institutions and bureaucracy, housing and vehicles, and long-horizon life admin. Nine units, thirty-seven experiences, closing in an integrated capstone. Every unit pairs worked examples with faded variants and cross-domain transfer exercises, so a skill learned in one context (negotiating a contractor quote) is explicitly re-applied in another (a salary conversation, a lease renewal). Six interleaving threads run the length of the course: negotiation, institutional escalation, research-attempt-escalate triage, time-value-of-money, habit architecture, and resilience. Roughly 10 hours of in-app time, plus about 5 hours of real-world practice across 9 self-guided exercises. Note: tax, insurance, credit, retirement, and housing content is written for a United States context; learners elsewhere should treat specific institutions, thresholds, and legal rules as illustrative. Figures that change annually (brackets, contribution limits, rates) are deliberately presented as illustrative, with learners directed to primary sources.

prerequisites.description: Basic reading comprehension and everyday arithmetic (percentages, simple multiplication and division).
No financial, legal, or fitness background required. Having a recent pay stub or bank statement on hand is helpful but optional for the practical activities.

interleavingGroups.rationaleNote: Contrasts the same anchor/BATNA/trade-off structure across four surface domains — a price or roommate negotiation, a salary ask, a lease renewal, and a contractor quote — to force near-transfer rather than domain-bound memorization.

Reuses the same escalation-ladder procedure first taught via a billing/tax dispute and then applied to a denied insurance claim and an out-of-network surprise bill, testing genuine transfer across different bureaucracies.

Interleaves the research → attempt → escalate triage framework across a home issue, a vehicle warning light, and a 'should I see a doctor?' decision so learners practice the same logic on mechanically different systems.

Repeatedly surfaces the same time-value-of-money reasoning across an emergency fund, a mortgage affordability calculation, a car-loan comparison, an employer-match calculation, and a major purchase — each at increasing stakes and decreasing scaffolding.

Applies the same environment-design and tiny-first-step structure to three physical habits (sleep schedule, weekly cooking, training) so 'systems over willpower' is practiced as a tool rather than memorized as a slogan.

Places resilience-reframing and regulation practice immediately after the course's failure-prone real-world tasks (negotiating, appealing, having a hard conversation) so the reframe has recent concrete experience to anchor to.

probeQuestions.prompt: If your take-home pay covers all your bills with money left over, do you still need to track your spending?
options: No — tracking is only for people overspending their income.
Yes — without visibility it's hard to see where the surplus actually goes or whether it's serving your goals.
No — budgets are purely restriction tools.
Only if you carry debt.

optionFeedback.feedback: Common assumption, but it overlooks the value of visibility into where surplus goes.
Correct — a budget's job is visibility and allocation, not just crisis management.
A budget is an allocation plan, not purely a restriction tool.
Cash-flow awareness is useful regardless of debt status.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as a prior-mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: Two friends each invest $200/month at the same return. One starts at 25, the other at 35, and both stop at 65. Roughly how do their ending balances compare?
options: About the same — the later starter only missed $24,000 of contributions.
The early starter ends with moderately more, maybe 10–20% extra.
The early starter ends with dramatically more — often close to double — because the earliest dollars compound the longest.
The later starter ends with more because markets were higher when they started.

optionFeedback.feedback: This counts contributions but ignores compounding — the missed decade is the most powerful one.
It's far larger than that. Exponential growth back-loads its gains, so the earliest years matter most.
Correct — time in the market is the ingredient you can't buy back later.
Start date within a long horizon matters far less than total time invested.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as a prior-mastery signal for the linked competencies.

probeQuestions.prompt: You carry a $3,000 balance on a credit card at 25% APR and pay only the minimum. What best describes what happens?
options: The balance shrinks steadily; minimums are designed to pay it off in a year or two.
Interest compounds against you, so most of each minimum payment goes to interest and the balance barely moves for years.
Nothing bad, as long as payments are on time your credit score protects you from interest.
The card company forgives interest once you've paid the original $3,000.

optionFeedback.feedback: Minimums are calculated to keep you in debt, not to get you out of it.
Correct — at ~25% APR, carried balances are one of the fastest ways to quietly lose money.
On-time payments protect your score, not your wallet — interest accrues on any carried balance.
Interest never stops accruing on a carried balance.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as a prior-mastery signal for the linked competencies.

probeQuestions.prompt: A raise pushes part of your income into a higher tax bracket. What happens to your take-home pay?
options: It can go down — the higher rate applies to all of your income.
It always goes up — only the dollars above the bracket line are taxed at the higher rate.
It stays the same — the raise and the tax cancel out.
It depends on whether you itemize deductions.

optionFeedback.feedback: This is the classic bracket myth — brackets are marginal, so only dollars above the line are taxed at the higher rate.
Correct — a raise can never lower your take-home pay through brackets alone.
The higher rate applies only to the marginal dollars, so the raise always nets positive.
Itemizing changes taxable income, but bracket mechanics are marginal either way.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as a prior-mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: Your employer matches 100% of 401(k) contributions up to 4% of your $60,000 salary. You contribute nothing. How much free money do you leave on the table each year?
options: $0 — the match only matters at retirement.
About $240
About $2,400
About $4,800

optionFeedback.feedback: The match is real compensation you forfeit every year you don't contribute.
Off by a factor of ten — recompute 60,000 × 0.04.
Correct — $2,400/year in guaranteed 100% return, before any market growth.
That would be an 8% match; this plan matches up to 4%.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as a prior-mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: Caffeine's half-life is roughly 5–6 hours. What does that imply about a 4 p.m. coffee if you sleep at 10 p.m.?
options: It's fully out of your system by bedtime.
Roughly half the caffeine is still active at bedtime, degrading sleep quality even if you fall asleep fine.
Caffeine only affects falling asleep, not sleep quality.
Half-life doesn't apply if you drink coffee daily.

optionFeedback.feedback: Half-life math says otherwise — about half is still circulating six hours later.
Correct — and degraded deep sleep is easy to miss because you still 'slept'.
Caffeine measurably reduces deep-sleep quality even when sleep onset feels normal.
Tolerance blunts the subjective buzz, not the pharmacokinetics.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as a prior-mastery signal for the linked competency.

probeQuestions.prompt: You receive a job offer at $70,000. Research says the role's market range is $75,000–$85,000. What is the strongest first move?
options: Accept immediately — negotiating risks the offer being withdrawn.
Counter with enthusiasm plus data: name the researched range, ask to get closer to it, then stop talking.
Demand the top of the range and threaten to walk.
Ask for a signing bonus instead; base salary is fixed.

optionFeedback.feedback: Withdrawn offers over a polite, data-backed counter are extremely rare; skipping the ask compounds into six figures over a career.
Correct — warm anchor, external data, then silence. This is the standard playbook.
Ultimatums burn goodwill; anchoring works without threats.
Base salary is usually the most negotiable and most compounding component.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as a prior-mastery signal for the linked competencies.

probeQuestions.prompt: Your health plan: $1,500 deductible, 20% coinsurance, $6,000 out-of-pocket maximum. A covered in-network procedure costs $10,000. Roughly what do you pay?
options: $1,500 — the deductible caps what you owe.
$3,200 — deductible plus 20% of the remaining $8,500.
$6,000 — costs always hit the out-of-pocket max.
$2,000 — 20% of the total bill.

optionFeedback.feedback: The deductible is where cost-sharing starts, not where it ends — coinsurance follows.
Correct — $1,500 + (0.20 × $8,500) = $3,200, under the $6,000 cap so the cap doesn't bind.
The out-of-pocket max is a ceiling, not a floor — here the math lands below it.
Coinsurance applies after the deductible, not to the whole bill.

scoringCriteria: Correct selection on first attempt counts as a prior-mastery signal for the linked competency.

competencies.title: Systems Over Willpower
competencies.description: Design environments and shrink first steps so starting is trivial, rather than relying on motivation. The meta-skill that makes every other skill in the course actually happen.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a stalled goal, redesign it into a trivially small first action plus an environment change that removes friction.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Learner produces a concrete first action under two minutes of effort and one environment modification, and explains why each reduces reliance on willpower.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: I just need more discipline — successful people force themselves to do hard things.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Behavioral research consistently shows environment and friction predict follow-through better than trait willpower; high performers engineer contexts where the good choice is the easy one.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner audit one failed habit and identify the friction points, then redesign the environment and retest for one week.

competencies.title: Emotional Regulation & Sitting With Discomfort
competencies.description: Treat feelings as information rather than commands: name emotions, let urges pass, and avoid making significant decisions during an emotional spike.

desiredOutcomes.description: In a charged scenario, identify and name the emotion, choose a delay tactic, and separate the feeling from the decision.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Learner's scenario response names the emotion, applies a concrete pause strategy, and defers the decision past the spike.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Strong feelings mean I need to act on them right now.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Urges reliably crest and pass; acting at the peak converts a temporary state into a lasting consequence. Naming the emotion measurably reduces its grip.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Practice the name-it-wait-it sequence on low-stakes irritations before applying it to consequential decisions.

competencies.title: Research → Attempt → Escalate Framework
competencies.description: A universal triage procedure for unfamiliar problems: research plausible causes, attempt a safe fix within skill and legal limits, and escalate to a professional when warranted. Reused across home, vehicle, health, and bureaucratic contexts.

desiredOutcomes.description: Apply the three-stage framework to a novel problem, correctly identifying the safe-attempt boundary and the escalation trigger.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Learner's plan sequences research before attempts, keeps attempts within stated safety/legal limits, and names a specific escalation condition.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Calling a professional first is always the responsible move.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Many common failures have safe, documented five-minute fixes; defaulting to professionals costs money and never builds diagnostic skill. The framework exists precisely to locate the real DIY/pro boundary.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk the learner through two contrast cases: a tripped breaker (safe attempt) versus a gas smell (immediate escalation), and have them articulate the distinguishing features.

competencies.title: Processing Rejection & Setbacks
competencies.description: Deliberately reinterpret failures — a rejected counter-offer, a denied appeal, a botched conversation — through an accurate, useful lens instead of a totalizing one.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a setback narrative, distinguish the factual event from the totalizing interpretation and produce a more accurate reframe with a next action.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Reframe separates fact from story, avoids both toxic positivity and catastrophizing, and ends in a concrete next step.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Reframing means telling yourself everything is fine.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Reframing is accuracy-seeking, not positivity-seeking: the goal is the most truthful useful interpretation, which sometimes includes 'that genuinely went badly, and here is what it does and doesn't mean.'
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Contrast three interpretations of the same rejection — catastrophizing, denial, and accurate reframe — and have the learner identify which claims each one makes that the evidence supports.

competencies.title: Sleep as Infrastructure
competencies.description: Treat 7–9 hours of consistent sleep as the base layer under mood, focus, weight, and long-term health: schedule consistency, sleep environment, and caffeine pharmacokinetics.

desiredOutcomes.description: Audit one's own sleep setup against evidence-based factors (consistency, light, temperature, caffeine timing) and produce a prioritized fix list.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Audit covers schedule consistency, environment, and caffeine cutoff, with fixes ranked by expected impact.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: I function fine on six hours, and coffee doesn't affect me anymore.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Chronic short sleep degrades performance while also degrading the ability to notice the degradation; caffeine tolerance blunts the subjective buzz but not the measurable disruption of deep sleep.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner run a one-week experiment extending sleep by 45 minutes and moving the caffeine cutoff earlier, tracking subjective energy against a baseline week.

competencies.title: Cooking a Real-Meal Rotation
competencies.description: Build a rotation of 5–6 reliable meals: proper seasoning, roasting vegetables, cooking a protein, and assembling components into varied bowls — for health, money, and hosting.

desiredOutcomes.description: Plan a personal 5-meal rotation with a consolidated grocery list and identify the technique each meal teaches.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Rotation covers at least one roast, one protein technique, and one assemble-from-components meal, with a realistic weekly cost estimate.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Cooking well requires talent and recipes followed exactly.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Home cooking is a small set of transferable techniques (season, sear, roast, taste) plus repetition; most bland results come from under-salting and crowded pans, not missing talent.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Assign the same simple dish cooked twice — once as written, once seasoned to taste in stages — and compare results.

competencies.title: Strength Training & Body Maintenance
competencies.description: Learn the compound movement patterns (squat, hinge, push, pull, carry), hip-hinging to protect the back, and the case for muscle as long-term health infrastructure.

desiredOutcomes.description: Identify the five movement patterns, describe a safe hip hinge, and outline a minimal 2-day beginner routine with progressive overload.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Routine covers all five patterns across the week, names a progression rule, and the hinge description keeps a neutral spine with the load close to the body.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Lifting is for people who want to get big; cardio is what health requires.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Muscle mass and strength independently predict healthy aging, injury resistance, and metabolic health; visible size requires years of deliberate effort and is not a side effect of basic training.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Present longitudinal outcomes tied to grip strength and muscle mass, then reframe training as buying future mobility rather than aesthetics.

competencies.title: Preventive Care & Knowing When to See a Doctor
competencies.description: Use free preventive visits, establish a primary care provider, and apply triage judgment to symptoms — including when self-care is fine and when delay is dangerous.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given symptom scenarios, correctly sort them among self-care, PCP visit, urgent care, and emergency care, citing the deciding features.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Sorting matches standard triage guidance and the learner names at least one red-flag feature per escalated case.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Going to the doctor when nothing is wrong is a waste of money.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Most plans cover preventive visits at no cost, and an established PCP relationship makes future care faster, cheaper, and better-informed; many costly conditions are cheap to catch early.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner verify their own plan's preventive coverage and compare the cost of an annual visit against one ER trip for something a PCP could have caught.

competencies.title: Cash-Flow Awareness & the Emergency Fund
competencies.description: Know your true monthly number, allocate surplus deliberately, and build a 3–6 month emergency fund in a high-yield account — the diagnostic step every other financial decision depends on.

desiredOutcomes.description: Compute a personal monthly baseline from real statements and derive a correctly-sized emergency fund target with a funding timeline.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Baseline distinguishes fixed from variable spending; fund target equals 3–6 months of essential expenses; timeline is arithmetic-consistent with stated surplus.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Budgets are restriction tools for people who overspend.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A budget is an allocation and visibility tool; without one, surplus leaks into unexamined spending and goals go unfunded even at healthy incomes.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner categorize one real month of transactions and compare discovered spending against their guessed spending.

competencies.title: Banking Products & Fee Structures
competencies.description: Match account types to jobs: checking for flow, high-yield savings for the emergency fund, and recognizing the real cost of fees and near-zero interest rates.

desiredOutcomes.description: Compare account options by APY and fee structure and quantify the annual difference for a given balance.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Comparison computes dollar-denominated annual differences correctly and selects the appropriate account per purpose.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: All savings accounts are basically the same.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: The spread between a 0.01% APY account and a ~4% APY HYSA on a $5,000 balance is roughly $200+ per year for identical effort and FDIC protection.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner compute the one-year interest difference on their own balance and, if material, open the better account as the unit's deliverable.

competencies.title: Compound Growth & Time-Value-of-Money Reasoning
competencies.description: The recurring quantitative spine of the course: exponential growth back-loads its gains, so time is the non-refundable input. Applied to emergency funds, mortgages, car loans, and retirement.

desiredOutcomes.description: Apply compounding reasoning across at least two distinct domains (e.g., a retirement start-age comparison and a loan-term comparison) with correct directional conclusions.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Learner's reasoning identifies time-in-system as the dominant variable and computes or estimates magnitudes within an order of magnitude.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Starting to invest ten years later just means missing ten years of contributions.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: It means missing the decade whose dollars compound the longest; in typical scenarios the late starter ends with roughly half the balance despite only ~25% fewer contributions.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner run the two-friends calculation themselves with a compound interest calculator and explain where the gap comes from.

competencies.title: Credit Cards, Scores & Reports
competencies.description: How scores are built (payment history, utilization), what they silently gate, and the pay-in-full discipline that makes a credit card a free tool instead of a trap.

desiredOutcomes.description: Explain the main score factors, pull and read one's own credit report, and state the pay-in-full rule and why utilization matters.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Explanation correctly weights payment history and utilization, and identifies at least one report section to check for errors.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Carrying a small balance helps your credit score.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Scores reward on-time payments and low reported utilization; carrying a balance adds interest cost with zero scoring benefit. Paying the statement in full achieves the same reporting.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Show a side-by-side of two identical profiles — one paying in full, one carrying $500 — with identical scores but divergent interest paid.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: As long as I pay on time, credit card debt is fine.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: On-time minimum payments protect the score while ~25% APR interest compounds against the balance; the score and the wallet are separate systems.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner compute total interest paid on a $3,000 balance at minimum payments versus a 12-month payoff plan.

competencies.title: Debt Prioritization & Payoff Strategy
competencies.description: Rank debts by interest rate and psychology (avalanche vs. snowball), understand minimum-payment mechanics, and build a payoff plan that survives contact with a real budget.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a multi-debt scenario, produce a prioritized payoff order with justification and a monthly plan consistent with the stated budget.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Order is defensible under avalanche or snowball logic (stated explicitly), and the plan's arithmetic is consistent.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: All debt is equally bad and should be avoided or killed at any cost.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Interest rate and terms matter enormously: 25% APR card debt is an emergency, while low-rate debt can rationally coexist with investing (e.g., capturing an employer match beats prepaying a 5% loan).
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner rank four debts by true annual cost and decide, with the match on the table, where the next $200/month goes.

competencies.title: Reading Contracts & Spotting Predatory Terms
competencies.description: Locate and evaluate the load-bearing clauses of everyday contracts — auto-renewal, arbitration, early-termination fees, variable rates — before signing.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a realistic consumer contract, identify the clauses that create ongoing obligations or asymmetric risk and flag any predatory patterns.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Learner locates auto-renewal, fee, and dispute-resolution clauses and correctly assesses which party each favors.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Contracts are boilerplate; nothing in them is negotiable or worth reading.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: The few load-bearing clauses (renewal, termination, fees, rate changes) vary widely and are frequently negotiable — and courts generally enforce what you signed regardless of whether you read it.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Give the learner a 15-minute skim protocol (find five clause types) and apply it to a real agreement they're currently party to.

competencies.title: Retirement Accounts & the Employer Match
competencies.description: 401(k) match capture, Roth vs. traditional mechanics, vesting, and the default playbook: capture the match, open a Roth IRA, buy broad index funds, ignore the noise.

desiredOutcomes.description: Compute the dollar value of a given employer match, choose Roth vs. traditional for a stated income scenario, and sequence the standard contribution priority order.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Match math is correct; Roth/traditional choice is justified by current-vs-future tax rate reasoning; priority order places match capture first.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Retirement saving can wait until I earn more; small contributions now don't matter.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: The match is an immediate 50–100% return forfeited every unclaimed year, and early small contributions dominate later large ones through compounding.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner compute their own forfeited match and the 40-year value of $100/month started today versus at 35.

competencies.title: Salary Negotiation
competencies.description: The highest-leverage negotiation most people never attempt: research-based anchoring, the warm counter, comfort with silence, and renegotiating every few years as raises compound off base.

desiredOutcomes.description: Draft a complete counter-offer for a realistic offer scenario: researched range, warm framing, specific ask, and a plan for the three most likely responses.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Counter cites external data, anchors above target, maintains warmth, and the response plan includes a walk-away/accept threshold.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Negotiating a first offer is risky and might make them rescind it.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Polite, data-backed counters are an expected part of hiring; rescissions over them are rare, while the unclaimed $5–10k compounds through every future raise and job change.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Reframe with base-rate evidence, then rehearse the counter aloud until the silence after the ask feels tolerable.

competencies.title: How Income Tax Actually Works
competencies.description: Marginal brackets versus effective rate, withholding, the standard deduction, and credits versus deductions — the conceptual model that kills the 'raise pushed me into a higher bracket' myth.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a salary and bracket table, compute tax owed, distinguish marginal from effective rate, and evaluate a raise's true after-tax value.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Computation applies rates per-bracket, not to the whole income, and correctly states that effective rate is always below marginal rate.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A raise can push you into a higher bracket and lower your take-home pay.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Brackets are marginal: only dollars above each threshold are taxed at the higher rate, so gross raises always increase net pay (benefit cliffs are a separate, unrelated phenomenon).
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner hand-compute tax on incomes $1 below and $1,000 above a bracket line and observe the take-home difference.

competencies.title: Insurance Coverage Evaluation
competencies.description: Premium, deductible, copay, coinsurance, and out-of-pocket maximum as one system; matching plan choice to actual risk; and reading what a policy does and doesn't cover before you need it.

desiredOutcomes.description: Compute total annual cost of two plan options under low-use and high-use scenarios and recommend one with justification.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Computation correctly sequences deductible → coinsurance → out-of-pocket max and includes premiums in the total-cost comparison.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The plan with the lowest premium is the cheapest plan.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Total cost is premium plus expected cost-sharing; low-premium/high-deductible plans can cost far more in a moderate-use year, and the right choice depends on realistic usage.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner run the same two health scenarios through both plan structures and compare annual totals.

competencies.title: Negotiation Fundamentals
competencies.description: The reusable core: anchoring, BATNA, interests versus positions, and trading concessions — practiced on everyday cases (prices, roommates) and transferred to leases, contractors, and salary.

desiredOutcomes.description: For a given scenario, identify both parties' interests, state a BATNA, set an anchor, and plan one concession trade.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Analysis distinguishes interests from positions and the anchor is justified by external reference points rather than hope.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Negotiation is adversarial — someone has to lose.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Most real negotiations are positive-sum: parties value things differently, so trading on those differences (timing, terms, extras) can improve both outcomes over the no-deal baseline.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Analyze a roommate-chores case where the naive 50/50 split loses to a trade based on differing preferences.

competencies.title: Hard Conversations & Boundaries
competencies.description: Saying the uncomfortable thing kindly and directly: the 'when X, I feel Y, I'd like Z' structure, 'no' as a complete sentence, and repairing after conflict.

desiredOutcomes.description: Script a real pending hard conversation using the observation-feeling-request structure and a repair plan if it goes badly.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Script opens with a specific observation rather than a character judgment, states a feeling without blame, and makes one concrete request.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Setting a boundary requires a justification long enough that the other person agrees with it.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Boundaries are decisions about your own behavior, not proposals requiring approval; over-justifying invites debate and signals the boundary is negotiable.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Practice compressing a three-paragraph justification into two kind sentences and rehearse holding steady after pushback.

competencies.title: Making & Keeping Adult Friendships
competencies.description: After school ends, friendship becomes a coordination problem: repeated low-stakes contact, being the initiator, and building systems (standing plans) that don't depend on spontaneity.

desiredOutcomes.description: Design a personal friendship-maintenance system: one recurring low-friction touchpoint and one initiation habit, with the systems-over-willpower logic applied.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: System includes a concrete recurring mechanism (standing plan, rotation, trigger-based check-in) rather than an intention to 'reach out more.'

commonMisconceptions.misconception: If they wanted to hang out, they'd reach out — always initiating means the friendship is one-sided.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Most adult friendship decay is logistical, not evaluative; nearly everyone waits by default, so initiators aren't lower-status — they're the ones who solve the coordination problem.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner audit which lapsed friendships ended from conflict versus scheduling drift, then test one re-initiation.

competencies.title: Escalating & Appealing Within Bureaucracies
competencies.description: The escalation ladder: document everything, ask for the supervisor or formal appeal channel, cite the specific rule, set deadlines, and know the external regulators — applied to billing disputes, tax notices, and claim denials.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given a wrongful bill or denial, produce a documented escalation plan: first contact script, evidence list, appeal deadline, and the external body if internal appeal fails.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Plan sequences internal channels before external ones, is dated and documented, and cites the specific policy or code at issue.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A denial or a bill from a big institution is final.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: First-line answers are frequently wrong or automated; a large share of appealed insurance denials are overturned, and billing errors are common — persistence through the documented ladder is the designed remedy.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk a real denied-claim case through internal appeal to state insurance regulator, noting the reversal points.

competencies.title: Filing Taxes Accurately
competencies.description: The practical mechanics on top of the conceptual model: W-2s and 1099s, free filing options, common credits for young adults, and what to do when a notice arrives.

desiredOutcomes.description: Assemble a personal filing checklist (documents, filing route, relevant credits) and correctly respond to a sample IRS notice scenario.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Checklist matches the learner's income situation; notice response reads the notice type, verifies against records, and responds through the stated channel by the deadline.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A big refund means you won at taxes.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A refund is repayment of an interest-free loan you gave the government through over-withholding; the win is accurate withholding and putting the difference to work all year.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner locate their withholding on a real pay stub and adjust their W-4 estimate against last year's actual liability.

competencies.title: Navigating the Health System
competencies.description: In-network versus out-of-network, reading an EOB (it's not a bill), urgent care versus ER economics, and disputing surprise bills.

desiredOutcomes.description: Read a sample EOB correctly, choose the right care setting for given scenarios, and outline the dispute path for a surprise out-of-network charge.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: EOB reading distinguishes billed, allowed, covered, and owed amounts; care-setting choices match urgency and cost logic; dispute path invokes the escalation ladder.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: An EOB is a bill I need to pay.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: An Explanation of Benefits is the insurer's accounting statement; the provider's bill comes separately, and discrepancies between the two are exactly what to check before paying anything.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Annotate a sample EOB and matching provider bill, having the learner reconcile the two and spot an intentional discrepancy.

competencies.title: Rent-vs-Buy & Housing Affordability
competencies.description: Down payments, PMI, closing costs, DTI, and the honest rent-vs-buy comparison including opportunity cost — reusing the time-value-of-money thread at higher stakes.

desiredOutcomes.description: Run a complete rent-vs-buy comparison for a given market scenario, including all ownership costs and the opportunity cost of the down payment.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Comparison includes PMI, closing costs, maintenance, and taxes on the buy side and invested-down-payment growth on the rent side, with a horizon-dependent conclusion.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Renting is throwing money away; buying always builds wealth.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Interest, taxes, insurance, maintenance, and transaction costs are also 'thrown away,' and the down payment has opportunity cost; over short horizons renting frequently wins the honest comparison.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner run the same scenario at 2-year and 10-year horizons and observe the conclusion flip.

competencies.title: Reading & Negotiating Leases
competencies.description: Security deposits, habitability rights, renewal and termination clauses, and what's actually negotiable — the tenant-side application of contract literacy and negotiation.

desiredOutcomes.description: Review a sample lease, flag problem clauses, list tenant rights that apply regardless of lease text, and draft one renewal-negotiation ask.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Review catches deposit, entry, and termination clauses; correctly notes that habitability rights can't be waived by lease language; negotiation ask uses external market data.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Whatever the lease says goes — signing it waives your rights.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Statutory tenant protections (habitability, deposit rules, notice requirements) override contrary lease clauses in most jurisdictions; unenforceable terms appear in leases anyway because tenants don't know this.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner identify two unenforceable clauses in a sample lease and cite the protection that overrides each.

competencies.title: Home Systems, Maintenance & Safety
competencies.description: The competence kit: breaker panel, water main shutoff, GFCI outlets, drain clearing, smoke/CO detectors — plus the legal and safety line where DIY ends and licensed work begins.

desiredOutcomes.description: Locate one's own breaker panel and water shutoff, demonstrate the triage framework on two common failures, and correctly classify a task list into DIY versus licensed work.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Classification places gas, structural, and most electrical work on the licensed side and articulates why.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: If a video shows how to do it, it's safe and legal for me to do it.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Gas, structural, and much electrical work legally require licensed professionals and permits in most jurisdictions, and errors can be invisible until catastrophic — video availability is not a safety or legality signal.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Sort a 12-task list into DIY/pro/permit categories against a jurisdiction checklist and discuss the misclassified items.

competencies.title: Vehicles: Maintenance, Diagnostics & True Cost
competencies.description: Routine maintenance (oil, tires, fluids), warning-light triage via the research-attempt-escalate framework, and total cost of ownership including depreciation and loan terms.

desiredOutcomes.description: Build a maintenance calendar for a given vehicle, triage three warning-light scenarios, and compare two purchase options on total cost of ownership.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: TCO comparison includes depreciation, financing, insurance, and maintenance — not just sticker price and monthly payment.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: A car I can afford is a car whose monthly payment fits my budget.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Monthly-payment framing hides loan term, interest, insurance, fuel, maintenance, and depreciation; a longer loan lowers the payment while raising the true cost and underwater-loan risk.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Have the learner compute five-year TCO for the same car at 36- and 72-month terms and compare against the payment-only view.

competencies.title: Vetting Contractors & Service Providers
competencies.description: Licensed/bonded/insured verification, multiple quotes, written scope of work, change orders, and payment scheduling — applying negotiation and contract literacy to hired work.

desiredOutcomes.description: Given three contractor quotes, verify credentials, compare scopes rather than bottom lines, and structure a payment schedule that protects the client.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Evaluation checks license/bond/insurance separately, normalizes quotes to a common scope, and avoids large upfront payments.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: The lowest quote is the best deal.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Quotes with different scopes aren't comparable; lowballs often exclude materials or permits and recover margin through change orders once the job is underway.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Normalize three deliberately-mismatched sample quotes to one scope and re-rank them.

competencies.title: Major Purchases & Opportunity Cost
competencies.description: A repeatable decision process for four-figure-plus choices: total cost framing, opportunity cost, avoiding monthly-payment anchoring, and cooling-off rules for emotional spikes.

desiredOutcomes.description: Apply the full decision process to a realistic major purchase, integrating TCO, opportunity cost, and an emotional-regulation checkpoint.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Analysis quantifies the next-best alternative, states total cost rather than payment, and includes a deliberate delay before commitment.

commonMisconceptions.misconception: If I can make the monthly payment, I can afford it.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: Payment-based framing is the seller's frame: it hides total cost, term length, and what the same dollars would do elsewhere — the opportunity cost that payment math never shows.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Reframe one real pending purchase in total-cost and opportunity-cost terms and compare the two framings' conclusions.

competencies.title: Essential Documents & Life Admin
competencies.description: A basic will, power of attorney, beneficiary designations, and a findable system for vital records — the small, boring setup that protects people you love from chaos.

desiredOutcomes.description: Produce a personal document checklist (what exists, what's missing, where each lives) and correctly explain what a will and POA each do.
desiredOutcomes.verificationCriteria: Checklist covers will, POA, beneficiaries, and record locations; explanations distinguish incapacity planning (POA) from estate distribution (will).

commonMisconceptions.misconception: Wills and powers of attorney are for older people with real assets.
commonMisconceptions.whyIncorrect: A POA governs incapacity at any age, beneficiary designations override wills on major accounts, and dying intestate hands decisions to state formulas — none of which depends on wealth.
commonMisconceptions.remediationStrategy: Walk through what actually happens to a 27-year-old's accounts, lease, and medical decisions with and without the basic documents in place.

sections.title: Unit 0 — Foundations: The Meta-Skills
sections.description: Four skills that determine whether anything else in this course actually happens: designing systems instead of relying on willpower, regulating emotions so they inform rather than command, a universal triage framework for unfamiliar problems, and processing setbacks accurately. Everything here gets reused — the frameworks in this unit reappear in money, health, housing, and negotiation contexts throughout the course.

learningObjectives.description: Redesign a stalled goal into a trivially small first action plus an environment change, and explain why each reduces reliance on willpower.
Name an emotion during a spike, apply a delay tactic, and separate the feeling from the decision it's pushing toward.
Apply the research → attempt → escalate framework to an unfamiliar problem, correctly locating the safe-attempt boundary and the escalation trigger.
Given a setback, separate the factual event from the totalizing interpretation and produce an accurate reframe with a next action.

motivationHooks.prompt: Most New Year's resolutions are dead by February. Before this unit: what do you think separates the people whose habits survive — stronger discipline, better goals, or something else entirely? Hold your answer; we'll come back to it.

Later units will ask you to open accounts, negotiate salaries, appeal insurance denials, and have hard conversations. Every one of those is a task people *know* they should do and don't. This unit is about closing that gap — it's the difference between finishing this course and abandoning it.

The answer to the opening prediction: it's *something else entirely*. Habit survivors don't out-discipline everyone — they design environments where the habit is the path of least resistance, shrink the first step until starting is trivial, and treat setbacks as data instead of verdicts. Discipline is what you need when your system is bad.

sectionSummary.content: You should now believe four things. **First**, follow-through is an engineering problem, not a character problem — friction and environment predict behavior better than motivation, so when a habit stalls, redesign the system instead of blaming yourself. **Second**, emotions are information, not commands: naming a feeling reduces its grip, urges crest and pass, and decisions made at the peak of a spike are decisions you'll often have to unmake. **Third**, unfamiliar problems yield to a repeatable triage: research plausible causes, attempt only what's safely and legally within reach, and escalate on specific triggers — a framework you'll reuse on outlets, engines, symptoms, and bureaucracies. **Fourth**, a setback is a fact plus a story; resilience is auditing the story for accuracy, not forcing positivity.
keyTakeaways: Shrink the first step until starting is trivial; make the good choice the easy one
Name the emotion, let the spike pass, then decide
Research → attempt (within safe limits) → escalate (on named triggers)
Separate what happened from what you're telling yourself it means

furtherReading.title: Atomic Habits
furtherReading.citation: Clear, J. (2018). Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones. Avery.
furtherReading.author: James Clear
furtherReading.rationaleNote: The most accessible full treatment of environment design, friction, and the two-minute rule — expands everything in the systems-over-willpower experience.

furtherReading.title: Tiny Habits
furtherReading.citation: Fogg, B. J. (2019). Tiny Habits: The Small Changes That Change Everything. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt.
furtherReading.author: BJ Fogg
furtherReading.rationaleNote: The research-lab version of the same ideas: behavior = motivation × ability × prompt, and why shrinking ability requirements beats pumping motivation.

furtherReading.title: Permission to Feel
furtherReading.citation: Brackett, M. (2019). Permission to Feel: Unlocking the Power of Emotions. Celadon Books.
furtherReading.author: Marc Brackett
furtherReading.rationaleNote: Evidence-grounded treatment of emotion labeling and regulation from the Yale Center for Emotional Intelligence — the science behind 'name it to tame it.'

furtherReading.title: Option B
furtherReading.citation: Sandberg, S., & Grant, A. (2017). Option B: Facing Adversity, Building Resilience, and Finding Joy. Knopf.
furtherReading.author: Sheryl Sandberg & Adam Grant
furtherReading.rationaleNote: Introduces the three P's of setback interpretation (personalization, pervasiveness, permanence) that the resilience experience builds on.

learningExperiences.title: The Motivation Myth: Systems Over Willpower
desiredCognitiveActions: Contrast willpower-based and system-based explanations of the same failed habit
Identify friction points in a real behavior chain
Generate a trivially small first action and an environment modification

contentBlocks.content:
## Why 'try harder' keeps failing

Here's a pattern you've probably lived: you decide to change something — exercise, cooking, saving, reading. Week one goes great. Week three, it's gone. The standard diagnosis is a character flaw: *I just don't have the discipline.*

That diagnosis is almost always wrong, and it's wrong in a useful way.

Behavioral research keeps converging on the same finding: **environment and friction predict follow-through better than motivation does.** People who appear disciplined mostly aren't resisting temptation better than you — they've arranged their lives so there's less to resist. The gym bag is already packed. The vegetables are pre-chopped. The savings transfer is automatic. The phone charges in the kitchen, not the bedroom.

Motivation is real, but it's weather — it comes and goes on its own schedule. A system is climate. You can't will yourself into wanting to cook at 7pm on a Tuesday after a bad day. You *can* make it so that on that Tuesday, cooking is genuinely the easiest available option.

contentBlocks.title: The direction of causality is backwards
contentBlocks.content: Most people wait to feel motivated before acting. But motivation mostly *follows* action — you feel like running about ten minutes into the run. This means the entire job of a good system is to get you through the first two minutes, after which the psychology starts working for you instead of against you.

terms.definition: Any small obstacle between intention and action; behavior change works by removing friction from good choices and adding it to bad ones.
Reducing a goal's opening step until it takes under two minutes, so starting costs almost nothing and momentum does the rest.
Arranging your physical and digital surroundings so the desired behavior is the default, lowest-effort path.

contentBlocks.content:
## The two moves

Every system redesign in this course comes down to two moves:

1. **Shrink the first action.** Not "go to the gym" — "put on shoes and step outside." Not "budget my finances" — "open the bank app and read one week of transactions." If the first step takes under two minutes, you've removed the negotiation with yourself that kills most attempts.
2. **Redesign the environment.** Ask: *where exactly does this behavior break down, and what one change removes that friction?* Then make the bad option harder at the same spot — the two directions compound.

The test of a good redesign is embarrassment: it should feel almost too small to count. That feeling is the signal you've actually removed the willpower requirement instead of relocating it.

rows: Shoes and clothes laid out the night before; alarm across the room; first action is *walk to the corner*, nothing more
One default meal with ingredients always stocked; delivery apps moved off the home screen; first action is *put the pan on the stove*
Automatic transfer on payday so saving requires zero decisions; spending money lives in a separate account
Phone charges in the kitchen; book on the pillow; first action is *read one page*
contentBlocks.caption: The same four goals, framed as character problems versus engineering problems.

interactionSequence.prompt: Two people both want to stop doomscrolling at night. Person A relies on self-control. Person B charges their phone in the kitchen and puts a book on the nightstand. Predict: after one month, how different are their outcomes, and why?

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: In studies and in practice, Person B wins decisively — not because they wanted it more, but because at the moment of decision (tired, in bed, willpower depleted) the environment already made the choice. Person A has to re-win the same battle every single night; Person B fought it once, at 4pm, by moving a charger.

interactionSequence.prompt: You've tried to start meal-prepping four times this year and quit within two weeks every time. What does the systems lens say to do *first*?
options: Recommit harder — write down your reasons and promise yourself this time is different
Find where exactly the behavior breaks down, then remove that specific friction and shrink the first step
Sign up for a paid meal-prep course so the sunk cost keeps you accountable
Accept that meal-prepping isn't for you

optionFeedback.feedback: This is attempt five of the same strategy that failed four times. Recommitment raises motivation temporarily; it changes nothing structural.
Correct — diagnose the exact failure point (Sunday overwhelm? no containers? recipe too ambitious?) and engineer that spot.
Sunk-cost pressure is just rented motivation — it decays like all motivation, usually before week three.
Maybe eventually — but four failures of a *willpower strategy* tell you nothing about whether a *system* would work. You haven't run that experiment yet.

interactionSequence.prompt: In your own words: pick one goal you've personally abandoned this year and explain, in two or three sentences, what the *willpower* explanation of the failure was — and what a *systems* explanation would be instead.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: A strong answer names a specific breakdown moment (a time of day, a decision point, a missing tool) rather than a trait. If your systems explanation still contains the words 'just need to' — it's a willpower explanation wearing a costume.

workedExamples.problem: Jordan has 'been meaning to' build an emergency fund for eight months. Every payday, the plan is to move whatever's left over into savings at the end of the month. There's never anything left over. Redesign this using systems-over-willpower.
steps: **Locate the breakdown point.** The transfer happens (a) at month-end, when the money is already gone, and (b) as a manual decision, which means it competes with every other use of the money every single month. Two friction points, both structural.
**Invert the order.** Pay-yourself-first: the transfer moves to payday, *before* discretionary spending, so saving no longer requires there to be money 'left over.'

**Remove the decision.** Set up an automatic transfer of a deliberately unscary amount — say $100 per paycheck — from checking to a separate high-yield savings account. Automatic means zero willpower spent, zero opportunities to skip.

**Shrink the first action.** The only real task is the one-time setup: open the HYSA and schedule the transfer. That's a 20-minute action, so schedule it like an appointment — *tonight, 7pm, laptop, couch*.
**Add friction to the failure mode.** Keep the savings account at a different bank with no debit card, so raiding it takes two days and deliberate effort instead of two taps.
workedExamples.conclusion: Notice what happened: Jordan's 'discipline problem' was solved with zero added discipline. The redesign moved the decision to a moment when money existed, then deleted the decision entirely, then made the failure mode annoying. Eight months of guilt versus 20 minutes of engineering. This exact pattern — automate the good default, add friction to the bad one — reappears in the retirement, debt-payoff, and budgeting units.

fadedVariant.problem: Sam wants to exercise three mornings a week. The current plan is 'wake up at 6:30 and decide between the gym, a run, or a video workout depending on how I feel.' Sam has worked out twice in six weeks. Apply the same redesign.
givenSteps: Locate the breakdown point: the decision happens at 6:30am — the worst possible moment — and it's actually *three* decisions (whether, what, where) stacked on top of each other.
Remove the decision: pick ONE default workout for all three days, decided the night before. Variety can come later; right now optionality is the enemy.
blankedStepPrompts: What is the shrunken first action — the under-two-minute step that counts as 'showing up'?
Name one environment change that makes the 6:30am good choice easier, and one that makes the failure mode (snoozing) harder.
blankedStepAnswers: Something like: 'put on the workout clothes laid out by the bed' or 'step out the front door.' The workout itself is not the first action — dressing is. Anyone who is dressed and outside at 6:32 almost always continues.

Easier: clothes and shoes staged the night before, coffee maker on a timer, workout video queued. Harder: phone/alarm charging across the room (standing up is now mandatory), or an accountability text scheduled to a friend at 6:45.

transferExercises.scenario: In Unit 2 you'll be asked to open a high-yield savings account and to categorize one month of real transactions. Historically, learners agree these are good ideas and then 60% never do them — the classic intention-action gap this experience is about.
transferExercises.task: Before Unit 2 arrives: apply both moves to *this course itself*. Write down (1) the trivially small first action for the HYSA task, (2) the exact time and place you'll do it, and (3) one friction you will remove in advance.
successCriteria: First action takes under two minutes (e.g., 'open a comparison page and bookmark one account' — not 'set up my savings')
Time and place are specific enough that a stranger could verify whether it happened
The removed friction is concrete (statement PDF already downloaded, ID card on the desk) rather than motivational

selfGuidedExercise.objective: Run a one-week systems redesign on a real stalled habit and evaluate the result against your willpower-based attempts.
selfGuidedExercise.realWorldContext: Any habit you've attempted and abandoned in the past year — exercise, cooking, sleep schedule, reading, budgeting.
instructions: Pick **one** stalled habit. Write one sentence on where exactly it broke down last time — the specific moment, not the general vibe.
Design the redesign: (a) a first action under two minutes, (b) one environment change that removes friction from the good choice, (c) one change that adds friction to the failure mode.
Implement all three **today** — the setup itself is the exercise, and it should take under 30 minutes.
Run it for seven days. Track only one thing: did the first action happen each day? (Not the full habit — just the first action.)
At the end of the week, compare: how many days did the first action fire versus your best willpower-based week?
materialsOrPrerequisites: Whatever physical setup the redesign needs (usually nothing you don't own)
reflectionPrompts: On days the first action fired, how often did the full behavior follow? What does that tell you about where the real barrier was?
Which mattered more for you — shrinking the first step, or the environment change? (People differ; knowing your lever is the payoff.)
Where else in your life is a 'discipline problem' that now looks like a design problem?

selfAssessmentCriteria: The first action genuinely takes under two minutes
The redesign was physically implemented, not just planned
Seven days of first-action data exist, even if imperfect

retrievalPractice.prompt: What are the two moves of a systems redesign, and what is the 'embarrassment test' for whether you've done the first one properly?
A friend says: 'I keep meaning to update my résumé but I never feel like it. I just need to force myself this weekend.' Diagnose the strategy and redesign it in two sentences.

learningExperiences.title: Feelings Are Data, Not Commands
desiredCognitiveActions: Distinguish an emotion from the action it urges
Apply labeling and delay tactics during a simulated spike
Evaluate a decision's quality separately from the feeling driving it

contentBlocks.content:
## The spike and the decision

An emotion is a fast, automatic summary of a situation: *threat*, *unfairness*, *loss*, *opportunity*. That summary is genuinely useful information. The problem is that emotions don't arrive as information — they arrive as **urges**: send the email, quit the job, buy the thing, cancel on everyone.

The skill is inserting a gap between the two. Not suppressing the feeling (that reliably backfires), not obeying it, but *reading* it:

- **Name it, specifically.** Not "I'm stressed" but "I'm embarrassed and a little scared about money." Affect labeling — literally putting feelings into words — measurably reduces the intensity of the emotional response. Precision matters: the more specific the label, the better it works.
- **Let the spike crest.** Emotional intensity is a wave, not a plateau. Urges feel permanent from inside but reliably peak and fall, usually within minutes to hours. What you're feeling at minute two is not what you'll be feeling at hour two.
- **Split the feeling from the decision.** The feeling can be 100% valid while the action it demands is 100% wrong. *Being right about the injustice* and *sending the 11pm email* are separate questions.

contentBlocks.title: The one hard rule
contentBlocks.content: **Don't make big decisions from an emotional spike.** Not because the feeling is wrong — often it's pointing at something real — but because spike-state thinking narrows to the immediate threat and discounts everything else: consequences, alternatives, other people's likely responses. If a decision is genuinely right, it will still be right tomorrow morning. Almost nothing is lost by waiting; a great deal is regularly lost by not waiting.

## Case: the 11pm email

Maya, 26, gets a Slack message at 10:40pm: her manager has reassigned the project she's led for four months to a colleague, effective Monday. No explanation, no heads-up, sent after hours.

Her chest is tight. She's typing before she's decided to type. The draft says things about respect, about the four months, about maybe it's time to look elsewhere. It is, sentence by sentence, entirely justified. Her finger is on send.

Walk through what each tool does here:

1. **Label:** *"I'm furious, and under that I'm hurt — this feels like being told I'm not valued."* Notice the label already contains information the anger alone didn't: the real issue is *valuation*, which is a conversation, not an email.
2. **Crest:** The message has been there for 20 minutes. It will still be there at 9am. Nothing about the situation requires a response tonight except the feeling itself.
3. **Split:** The feeling says *this was handled badly* — probably true, worth addressing. The urge says *make them feel it right now* — which converts a strong position (wronged, professional, owed an explanation) into a weak one (the person who sent the unhinged 11pm email).

Maya's best move is boring: save the draft, sleep, and at 9:30am request a conversation — where the *when X, I feel Y, I'd like Z* structure from Unit 5 will do more damage to the actual problem than the email ever could.

interactionSequence.prompt: Before reading on: Maya sends the 11pm email. Predict the three most likely consequences over the following two weeks — for the project decision, for her manager's view of her, and for her own position.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: Typical trajectory: the project decision doesn't change (it was already made, and midnight emails don't reopen decisions — meetings do); the manager's story shifts from 'I handled that badly' to 'and this is why'; and Maya now starts the real conversation apologizing for the email instead of holding the high ground. The cruel asymmetry: the *feeling* was justified, and the *action* still made everything worse. That asymmetry is the whole lesson.

interactionSequence.prompt: Which statement best captures the relationship between an emotion's *validity* and the urged action's *wisdom*?
options: If the feeling is justified, acting on it is justified — suppressing valid anger is dishonest
Feelings are irrational noise; mature people decide as if they felt nothing
They're independent questions: a fully valid feeling can urge a self-defeating action, so you honor the feeling as data and evaluate the action on its own merits
The stronger the feeling, the more it should count in the decision

optionFeedback.feedback: This fuses the two questions — and it's exactly the fusion that had Maya's finger on send. Addressing the issue ≠ obeying the urge.
Overcorrection. The anger contained real information (the valuation issue); discarding it entirely would lose the signal along with the noise.
Correct — validity of the feeling and wisdom of the action are separate audits, and running them separately is the skill.
Intensity tracks how threatened you feel, not how correct the urged action is — spike intensity is arguably a *reason for more* delay, not more weight.

interactionSequence.prompt: Think of the last time you sent, said, or bought something during a spike. In your own words: what was the feeling, what was it *correctly* telling you, and what would 'honor the data, skip the urge' have looked like?

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The most useful part of this exercise is the middle question — finding what the feeling was *right about*. Regulation isn't about concluding your emotions are wrong; it's about extracting their information at a time and in a form of your choosing. Notice this connects to the resilience experience coming up: there too, the goal is accuracy, not positivity.

transferExercises.scenario: Unit 8 covers major purchases, where sellers deliberately engineer emotional spikes: limited-time pricing, 'someone else is looking at it,' the test drive glow. The spike-decision gap you just learned is the primary defense.
transferExercises.task: Write yourself a two-sentence 'spike protocol' for purchases over $500 — a rule you set now, while calm, that governs future-you during the spike. Make it mechanical enough to follow while excited.
successCriteria: Protocol includes a mandatory delay with a specific duration (e.g., 48 hours, one paycheck)
Protocol is unconditional — no 'unless it's a really good deal' escape hatch, since the spike controls what feels like a really good deal
Includes one concrete check performed during the delay (compare total cost, check the opportunity cost, ask one person)

retrievalPractice.prompt: What does affect labeling do to emotional intensity, and why does the *specificity* of the label matter?
You get a lowball offer on something you're selling and feel insulted. The urge is to reply with something cutting. Run the three-step sequence (label, crest, split) on this in three sentences.

learningExperiences.title: Research → Attempt → Escalate: One Framework for Every Unfamiliar Problem
desiredCognitiveActions: Sequence the three stages correctly on a novel problem
Locate the safe-attempt boundary using safety, legality, and reversibility
Name specific escalation triggers before starting, not after getting stuck

contentBlocks.content:
## Adults are not people who know everything

A dead outlet. A car warning light. A weird letter from the IRS. A rash. The defining feature of adult problems is that most of them are *unfamiliar* — nobody taught you this specific one, and nobody is coming to handle it.

The two default responses are both expensive. **Panic-hire** — calling a professional for everything — costs money constantly and never builds judgment. **Panic-DIY** — attempting everything a video suggests — occasionally burns the house down. The skill isn't knowing the answers; it's a *procedure* for approaching anything unknown:

### 1. Research
Spend 15–30 minutes understanding the problem before touching anything. What are the common causes, cheapest first? What do the manufacturer, the manual, or reputable guides say? Crucially: **what does this problem look like when it's dangerous?** You're not trying to become an expert — you're trying to sort the situation into *trivial*, *attemptable*, or *professional*.

### 2. Attempt
Try the safe, reversible, legal fixes — and only those. Three questions define the boundary:
- **Safety:** Can a mistake here injure someone or cause damage that isn't obvious until later? (Electricity behind the panel, gas, anything structural: yes.)
- **Legality:** Does this work require a license or permit in my jurisdiction? (Much electrical, gas, and structural work does.)
- **Reversibility:** If my attempt fails, is the situation the same as before, or worse?

### 3. Escalate
Hand it to a professional — *on triggers you named in advance*. "Escalate if the breaker trips again after reset" decided at the start is a plan; the same decision made after four hours of frustrated fiddling is a rescue.

contentBlocks.title: Some problems skip straight to escalate
contentBlocks.content: The research step's first job is checking for **immediate-escalation signals**: gas smell, burning smell or scorch marks, sparking, water near electrical, structural movement, or (in the health version) red-flag symptoms like chest pain or one-sided weakness. For these, the framework collapses to one step: get the professional, now. Research–attempt is for everything *below* that line — and knowing where the line is *is* the competency.

rows: Manual, breaker map, reputable how-to for *this* symptom
Reset breaker, GFCI reset button, plunge/snake a drain, replace a bulb or filter
Repeated tripping, burning smell, anything behind the panel or in the walls, gas, structural
Owner's manual for the specific light; is it steady or flashing?
Check oil, coolant, tire pressure; tighten gas cap for an evap code
Flashing check-engine light, brake or oil-pressure warnings, anything while driving that affects control
Symptom checkers from reputable medical sources; red-flag list first
Rest, hydration, OTC meds per label, monitoring with a written log
Red-flag symptoms; anything severe, sudden, or worsening; persistence past the guideline window
Read the actual letter/denial code; find the official process
One clarifying call, documented; a correctly-filed form or appeal
Deadlines approaching, sums that justify professional help, legal language you can't parse
contentBlocks.caption: Same framework, four surfaces. Units 6, 7, and the health unit each get their own deep version of one row.

interactionSequence.prompt: Your apartment's bathroom outlet stops working. Order these actions from *first* to *last* (one of them should never happen — put it last).
options: Check whether other outlets are also dead, and look for a GFCI reset button on nearby outlets
Check the breaker panel for a tripped breaker
Search the symptom to understand common causes and danger signs
Call the landlord/electrician because the outlet still doesn't work and shows scorch marks
Unscrew the outlet cover and probe the wiring to see if a wire is loose

feedback.correctiveFeedback: Research first (30 seconds of reading tells you GFCI outlets exist and that scorch marks mean stop). Then the two safe attempts — GFCI reset and breaker check — both designed for users, both fully reversible. Scorch marks are an immediate-escalation signal. And opening the outlet is on the wrong side of all three boundary questions: unsafe (live wiring), often illegal in a rental, and non-reversible if you get it wrong. It stays last — meaning never.
hints: One option is a named immediate-escalation signal from the callout above.
Two options are explicitly designed to be user-operated resets.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you in this ordering?

interactionSequence.prompt: In your own words: why does naming the escalation trigger *before* attempting anything produce better decisions than deciding to escalate 'when it feels stuck'?

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: Two reasons worth having said: (1) pre-named triggers are set while you're calm and objective — mid-problem, sunk cost and frustration distort the call in both directions (quitting too early or fiddling too long); (2) a trigger converts escalation from a personal failure ('I gave up') into plan execution ('the trip-again condition fired'). Note the echo of the emotional-regulation experience: in both, the move is making the decision *before* the spike.

workedExamples.problem: Tuesday morning, your car's check-engine light comes on. The car drives normally. You know nothing about cars. Apply the framework.
steps: **Research (10 min):** Owner's manual first: a *steady* check-engine light means 'have it checked soon'; a *flashing* one means 'active misfire — stop driving soon and get service now.' Yours is steady. Common benign cause per the manual and reputable guides: a loose gas cap triggering the evaporative-emissions sensor.

**Define the attempt boundary:** Tightening a gas cap is safe, legal, and reversible — clearly attemptable. Anything involving sensors, codes, or parts is on the other side (for now; an OBD-II reader is a $25 future upgrade that moves the boundary).
**Attempt:** Tighten the gas cap until it clicks. Drive normally; the light typically clears within a few days of driving if that was the cause.

**Pre-name the escalation triggers:** (a) light still on after ~3 days of driving → mechanic appointment; (b) light starts flashing, or any performance change — rough idle, power loss, new noise → service *now*, not in three days.

**Escalate on trigger, with leverage:** If it goes to the mechanic, the research step already paid off — you can say 'steady check-engine light, five days, gas cap didn't clear it, no performance changes,' which marks you as a customer who can't be upsold with vague alarm.
workedExamples.conclusion: Total cost if it was the gas cap: $0 and two minutes. Total cost of panic-hiring the same problem: a diagnostic fee plus whatever vague alarm sells you. Total cost of ignoring it: possibly a catalytic converter. The framework isn't about being handy — it's about spending 10 minutes to sort the situation correctly.

fadedVariant.problem: Your kitchen sink is draining slower every day and now stands with an inch of water. You rent. Apply the framework.
givenSteps: Research: slow-then-standing water in one fixture points to a local clog (the drain or trap), not a main line — main-line problems show up in multiple fixtures at once. Chemical drain cleaners are widely advised against (pipe damage, and a hazard for whoever works on it next).
blankedStepPrompts: What are the safe attempts, in order, and what makes them attemptable under the three boundary questions?
What are your pre-named escalation triggers — and what does the fact that you *rent* change about who you escalate to?
blankedStepAnswers: Plunge the sink (safe, legal, reversible), then clean the P-trap under the sink with a bucket beneath it (hand-removable on most sinks, fully reversible), then a hand drain snake. Each is user-scale, requires no permit, and leaves the situation no worse if it fails.

Triggers: water backing up into *other* fixtures (main-line signal — stop immediately), the clog persisting after trap cleaning and snaking, or anything requiring wall/pipe access. Renting changes the escalation target: it goes to the landlord in writing, not to a plumber you hire — drain infrastructure is habitability territory, and Unit 7 covers how to document that request.

transferExercises.scenario: A letter arrives from your health insurer: a claim from two months ago is denied, code 'service not covered,' amount $840. Unit 6 will teach the full appeal ladder — but the framework you just learned already generates most of the right first moves.
transferExercises.task: Without any insurance knowledge, write the research-attempt-escalate plan for this letter: what you'd research, what a safe 'attempt' looks like for a bureaucratic problem, and what triggers escalation (and to whom).
successCriteria: Research includes reading the denial code's actual meaning and the plan's coverage language — the document equivalent of reading the manual
Attempt is a documented clarifying call and/or the standard internal appeal — reversible, free, and user-scale
Escalation triggers include the appeal deadline and a second denial; escalation target is the external review / state regulator (naming 'some higher authority exists' is sufficient before Unit 6)

retrievalPractice.prompt: Name the three boundary questions that separate a safe attempt from an escalation, and give one example of a problem that skips straight to escalate.
Contrast 'panic-hire' and 'panic-DIY' as failure modes: what does each cost, and what single habit does the framework replace them both with?

learningExperiences.title: Setbacks Without Stories: Accurate Reframing
desiredCognitiveActions: Separate the factual event from the interpretation layered onto it
Audit an interpretation against the three P's (personal, pervasive, permanent)
Produce a reframe that is more accurate — not merely more positive — and ends in a next action

contentBlocks.content:
## A setback is a fact plus a story

This course is going to ask you to do things that can visibly fail: counter a salary offer, appeal a denial, raise a hard topic with a roommate. Some of those attempts *will* fail — the skills raise your batting average, they don't make you unrejectable. So the last foundation skill is what happens after a no.

Here's the anatomy. Something happens: *the counter-offer was declined.* That's the fact — usually one sentence, boring, undeniable. Then, within seconds, a story grows around it: *I overreached. They probably regret hiring me. I'm bad at negotiating. I should never have asked.* The story feels like perception, but it's authorship — and it's the story, not the fact, that determines what you do next.

Three properties make a story toxic, and they're checkable:

- **Personal** — *this happened because of what I am* (vs. what was done, or circumstances)
- **Pervasive** — *this contaminates everything* (vs. this one domain, this one attempt)
- **Permanent** — *this is how it will always go* (vs. this is how it went once)

Reframing is not swapping a sad story for a happy one. It's **auditing the story against the evidence** — asking what the facts actually support. Sometimes the accurate story includes real fault: *I did anchor too high, and next time I'll cite the range first.* Accurate-with-fault beats flattering-and-vague, because only the accurate version contains instructions.

contentBlocks.title: One rejection, three stories
contentBlocks.content:
**Fact:** You asked for $80k; they said the budget is fixed at $72k.

**Catastrophizing:** *I blew it — they think I'm arrogant now. I'm lucky they didn't pull the offer.* (Personal, pervasive, permanent — and evidence-free: they said the budget is fixed, nothing else.)

**Toxic positivity:** *Everything happens for a reason! Their loss!* (Pleasant, but it also ignores the evidence — and contains no instructions.)

**Accurate reframe:** *The ask was right to make; the base was genuinely fixed.
