Note the honest caveat you should keep: this assumes you're not neglecting higher-priority steps like a starter emergency fund or crushing high-interest debt (Unit 3) — compounding is powerful, but 25% credit-card interest compounds against you even faster.

workedExamples.problem: Make the 'start early' claim concrete. Compare Alex (invests $300/month from 25–35, then stops) and Blake (invests $300/month from 35–65) at 7% annual growth, and explain the result.
steps: **Set up the two paths.** Alex: 10 years of contributions (age 25–35), $36,000 total in, then 30 years of untouched growth to 65. Blake: 30 years of contributions (age 35–65), $108,000 total in, growing to 65.

**Give Alex's money time to work.** Alex's $36,000 stops going in at 35 but keeps compounding for 30 more years. At ~7%, money roughly doubles about every decade — so Alex's early balance doubles roughly three times after contributions stop.

**Compare the endpoints.** When you run the numbers, Alex ends up around the same as — often ahead of — Blake at 65, despite contributing a third as much, purely because Alex's dollars compounded for more years.

**Locate the lever.** The difference isn't contributions (Blake wins that 3-to-1) or returns (identical 7%). It's *time*. Alex's earliest dollars each got up to 40 years to grow; Blake's latest dollars got only a handful.
**Draw the rule.** Therefore: start as early as you can, even small. The years you have now are the highest-value inputs you'll ever put in — and unlike contributions, you can't add them back later.
workedExamples.conclusion: This single comparison is why Unit 4 will push you to capture an employer match and open a retirement account *now* rather than 'once you earn more.' The exact figures depend on returns nobody can guarantee, but the direction is rock-solid across any steady positive rate: early dollars dominate. Time is the one ingredient this course can't teach you to make more of — only to stop wasting.

fadedVariant.problem: Apply the same time-value reasoning in reverse, to *borrowing*. A friend is choosing between a 3-year and a 6-year car loan; the 6-year has a lower monthly payment. Use compounding logic to explain the catch.
givenSteps: Recognize that the same force that grows your investments — interest compounding over time — works against you when you borrow: more time means more total interest paid.
Note that the lower monthly payment on the 6-year loan is achieved by stretching the balance over more time, not by making the car cheaper.
blankedStepPrompts: Explain what the lower monthly payment is hiding, in time-value terms — where does the 'saved' money actually go?
Connect this back to the Alex/Blake investing example: what's the symmetry between compounding *for* you and *against* you?
blankedStepAnswers: The lower payment hides a higher *total cost*: over six years you pay interest for twice as long, so you pay substantially more overall even though each month feels cheaper. The 'savings' isn't saved — it's spread out and enlarged by extra years of interest, and you also risk owing more than the car is worth for longer. Payment-size framing is the seller's frame; total cost over time is the real one (a point Unit 8 develops).

The symmetry is that time amplifies compounding in whichever direction it points. For an investor, more time turns small early dollars into large sums — time is your ally. For a borrower, more time turns a given balance into more total interest paid — time is your opponent. Same exponential engine; the sign flips depending on whether you own the asset or owe the debt.

transferExercises.scenario: Unit 4 will ask you to capture an employer 401(k) match and open a Roth IRA. The whole urgency of 'do it now, even small' rests on the compounding logic you just learned.
transferExercises.task: Using a free online compound-interest calculator, run your own version of the start-early comparison: pick a monthly amount you could plausibly invest, and compare starting today versus starting in 10 years, both growing to age 65 at ~7%. Write down the two ending numbers and the gap.
successCriteria: Uses realistic personal inputs (a monthly amount you could actually commit) rather than abstract ones
Correctly holds everything constant except start date, isolating the effect of time
States the dollar gap and draws the explicit conclusion for their own Unit 4 decisions

retrievalPractice.prompt: Explain the time value of money and why exponential (compounding) growth makes the earliest dollars invested the most valuable.
Contrast how time affects an investor versus a borrower: in one sentence each, how does compounding over more years help the investor and hurt the borrower?

sections.title: Unit 3 — Credit, Debt & Consumer Self-Defense
sections.description: The defensive half of personal finance: how credit scores are actually built, why a credit card is either a free tool or a 25% trap depending on one habit, how to rank debts so your money kills the most expensive one first, and how to read the handful of clauses in a contract that will actually cost you. This unit deliberately follows Money Foundations — you need your real monthly number and a starter cushion before a payoff plan means anything — and precedes Growing Wealth, because high-interest debt compounds against you faster than almost anything can compound for you.

learningObjectives.description: Explain the main credit score factors, read a credit report for errors, and state what a score silently gates.
Apply the pay-in-full rule and explain why carrying a balance costs interest without buying any scoring benefit.
Given multiple debts, produce a prioritized payoff order with an explicit strategy and a plan consistent with a real budget.
Locate and evaluate the load-bearing clauses of an everyday contract — renewal, termination, fees, dispute resolution — and identify which party each favors.

motivationHooks.prompt: A $3,000 credit card balance at 25% APR, paying only the minimum each month. Guess two numbers before this unit: how many years until it's paid off, and how much total interest you'd pay. Write them down — almost everyone's guesses are dramatically too low.

Your credit score silently gates apartments, car loans, mortgage rates, insurance pricing in some states, and occasionally jobs — and the difference between a good and mediocre score can be tens of thousands of dollars over a mortgage. Meanwhile the average carried card balance costs more per year in interest than most people's entire grocery budget. This is the unit where money stops leaking.

Why the guesses come in low: minimum payments are calculated to keep the debt alive, not to retire it — a large share of each payment goes to interest, so the balance barely moves for years. That $3,000 can take well over a decade and cost more in interest than the original balance. Minimums aren't a payoff plan; they're a subscription to your own debt.

sectionSummary.content: You should now believe four things. **First**, a credit score is built mostly from paying on time and keeping reported utilization low — and carrying a balance buys you interest, not points. Your report is worth pulling free once a year, because errors are common and they cost you real money. **Second**, a credit card is a genuinely free tool if and only if you pay the *statement balance in full* every month; the moment you carry a balance at ~25% APR, compounding switches sides and works against you faster than almost any investment works for you. **Third**, debts are not equal: rank them by interest rate, capture any employer match first (a guaranteed 100% return beats every rate on your list), attack the highest-APR debt next, and leave low-rate loans at minimums. **Fourth**, contracts are read in 15 minutes by hunting five clause types — auto-renewal, termination and fees, rate changes, dispute resolution, and what happens on default — because those are the ones that will ever actually cost you.
keyTakeaways: Pay the statement balance in full — every month, no exceptions, no 'balance for the score'
Score = mostly on-time payments + low utilization; pull your free report and check for errors
Rank debts by rate: match first, highest APR next, low-rate loans stay at minimums
Skim contracts for five clauses: renewal, termination/fees, rate changes, disputes, default

furtherReading.title: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau — Credit Reports & Scores
furtherReading.citation: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. Credit reports and scores.
furtherReading.author: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau
furtherReading.rationaleNote: The neutral, ad-free U.S. government explainer on scores, reports, and disputing errors — worth trusting over the credit-monitoring industry that profits from your anxiety.

furtherReading.title: AnnualCreditReport.com
furtherReading.citation: AnnualCreditReport.com — the federally authorized source for free credit reports.
furtherReading.rationaleNote: The only federally authorized free source for your reports from the three major bureaus. Use this for the self-guided exercise rather than any site that asks for a card number.

furtherReading.title: The Psychology of Money — on debt and margin for error
furtherReading.citation: Housel, M. (2020). The Psychology of Money: Timeless Lessons on Wealth, Greed, and Happiness. Harriman House.
furtherReading.author: Morgan Housel
furtherReading.rationaleNote: Useful counterweight to pure-math payoff advice: makes the behavioral case for why the plan you'll actually finish can beat the mathematically optimal one you abandon.

furtherReading.title: Federal Trade Commission — Consumer Advice
furtherReading.citation: Federal Trade Commission. Consumer Advice.
furtherReading.author: Federal Trade Commission
furtherReading.rationaleNote: Government guidance on negative-option/auto-renewal practices, debt collection rights, and common predatory patterns — the reference behind the contract-literacy experience.

learningExperiences.title: What a Credit Score Actually Measures
desiredCognitiveActions: Rank the score factors by actual weight rather than folklore
Distinguish a credit report from a credit score
Identify what a score gates and what it costs to have a mediocre one

contentBlocks.content:
## A number that follows you around

Your credit score is a lender's shorthand for one question: *if we lend this person money, do they pay it back on time?* It's not a measure of wealth, income, or virtue — plenty of high earners have mediocre scores and plenty of modest earners have excellent ones.

What makes it worth your attention is how quietly it gates things. A landlord runs it before approving your apartment. A lender uses it to set your mortgage *rate*, which over 30 years is a difference measured in tens of thousands of dollars. Auto lenders price loans off it. In some states insurers use credit-based scores in pricing. Occasionally an employer checks a version of your report.

None of those doors announce themselves. You just get approved or you don't, and get the good rate or the expensive one — which is exactly why this is worth ten minutes now rather than a surprise at the worst moment.

## What actually moves it

Scoring models differ in the details, but the weighting is broadly consistent and two factors dominate:

1. **Payment history — the biggest factor by far.** Did you pay on time? A single payment reported 30+ days late can drop a good score substantially and linger for years. Everything else is a rounding error next to this.
2. **Utilization — the second biggest.** What share of your available credit are you using, as reported? Lower is better. This is the one you can move *fast* — it's recalculated as balances get reported, unlike history, which only heals with time.
3. **Age of accounts** — longer is better, which is why closing your oldest card can backfire.
4. **Credit mix and new applications** — minor factors. Each new application causes a small, temporary dip.

The practical upshot: **automate every minimum payment** so factor one is never damaged by forgetfulness, and **keep reported balances low** relative to limits. That's most of the game.

contentBlocks.title: Report ≠ score
contentBlocks.content: Your **report** is the underlying record — accounts, balances, payment history, inquiries. Your **score** is a number calculated *from* that report. This distinction matters because errors live in the report and silently drag the score: an account that isn't yours, a payment marked late that wasn't, a closed card still showing a balance. Reports are free at AnnualCreditReport.com — the federally authorized source, not the sites that want a card number — and errors are common enough that checking once a year is genuinely worth the twenty minutes. Disputing an error is a documented process, which is to say: it's the Unit 0 escalation ladder, pointed at a credit bureau.

terms.definition: The underlying record of your credit accounts, balances, payment history, and inquiries, maintained by the credit bureaus — the raw data your score is computed from.
The record of whether you've paid accounts on time; the single largest factor in a credit score.
A lender's credit check when you apply for credit, causing a small temporary score dip; distinct from a soft inquiry (like checking your own score), which has no effect.
Having little or no credit history, which itself makes approval harder — avoiding credit entirely is not a neutral choice.

interactionSequence.prompt: Order these by how much they influence a typical credit score, from *most* influential to *least*.
options: Whether you pay your accounts on time
How much of your available credit you're using
How long your accounts have been open
Whether you have a mix of card and loan types

feedback.correctiveFeedback: Payment history dominates — protect it by automating every minimum payment, forever. Utilization is second and is the fastest lever you control, since it updates as balances get reported. Age of accounts matters moderately and is why closing your oldest card can hurt. Credit mix is a minor factor and never worth taking on a loan you don't need. Notice what's absent from the whole list: your income, your savings, and whether you carry a balance.
hints: One factor is so dominant that the others combined don't match it — and it's the one that's damaged by simple forgetfulness.
One factor is the fastest to change because it's recalculated as balances report each month.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you in this ranking?

interactionSequence.prompt: You've never had a credit card or loan because you dislike debt. What's the effect on your credit?
options: Excellent score — no debt means no risk, which is what lenders want to see
A thin file: with little history to score, you may be denied apartments or get worse rates than someone with a modest, well-managed history
No effect either way; scores only start when you borrow
Your score defaults to average until you take on credit

optionFeedback.feedback: Lenders can't see 'never risky' — they see no evidence at all, which isn't reassuring to them.
Correct — avoiding credit isn't neutral; it leaves you unable to demonstrate the one thing scoring measures.
It has a real effect: the gates a score opens (apartments, rates) stay closed or expensive without a history.
There's no neutral default — a thin file is scored as unknown risk, which lenders price conservatively.

interactionSequence.prompt: In your own words: why is checking your credit *report* a different (and arguably more useful) activity than checking your credit *score*?

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The distinction worth having restated: the score is an output — watching it tells you *that* something is wrong but never *what*. The report is the input, and it's where actionable problems live: an account you don't recognize (possible identity theft), a late mark that's incorrect, a balance that should be zero. You can't dispute a score; you dispute an item on a report. Free score apps are fine for monitoring, but the annual report pull is the one that can actually find and fix money-costing errors.

transferExercises.scenario: Unit 6 teaches the full escalation ladder for bureaucracies. A credit report error is a perfect small-stakes rehearsal: a large institution has a wrong fact about you, and there's a documented process for fixing it that most people never use.
transferExercises.task: Without needing Unit 6's details yet, sketch the research → attempt → escalate plan for a hypothetical incorrect late payment on your report: what would you research, what's the documented 'attempt', and what would trigger escalating further?
successCriteria: Research: identify which bureau(s) report the error and what documentation would disprove it (statements, payment confirmations)
Attempt: file the formal dispute with the bureau and/or the furnisher, in writing, keeping dated records
Escalation trigger named (dispute rejected or ignored past the response window) with a sense that an external regulator exists — the CFPB — even before Unit 6 names it

selfGuidedExercise.objective: Pull your actual credit report and audit it for errors.
selfGuidedExercise.realWorldContext: Your real credit file at the major bureaus, via the federally authorized free source.
instructions: Go to AnnualCreditReport.com — the federally authorized site — and request your free report. Be wary of lookalike sites that ask for a credit card.
Read the account list first: does every account belong to you? An unrecognized account is the highest-priority finding on the page.
Check the payment history on each account for late marks you don't believe are accurate.
Check balances and limits — a closed card still showing open, or a wrong limit (which inflates your utilization), are common errors.
Note anything wrong. If you find an error, file the dispute — that's the escalation ladder in action, and it's free.
materialsOrPrerequisites: Identification details for the bureaus' verification questions
reflectionPrompts: Did anything on the report surprise you — an account you'd forgotten, a limit you misremembered?
What's your reported utilization across all cards, and is it lower or higher than you'd have guessed?
If you found nothing wrong, what's your plan for making this an annual habit rather than a one-time curiosity?

selfAssessmentCriteria: The report was pulled from the authorized free source, not a paid lookalike
Every listed account was actually checked for ownership and accuracy
Any error found has a dispute filed or scheduled

retrievalPractice.prompt: Name the two dominant credit score factors in order, and state the single habit that protects the larger of the two.
What's the difference between a credit report and a credit score, and why does the distinction matter when something is wrong?

learningExperiences.title: Free Tool or 25% Trap: One Habit Decides
desiredCognitiveActions: Separate the score system from the interest system
Reason about compounding working against you
Kill the 'carry a small balance for your score' folklore with a concrete comparison

contentBlocks.content:
## The same card, two completely different products

A credit card is not one thing. Depending on a single habit, it's either:

- **A free tool** — you buy things you were going to buy anyway, get fraud protection the debit card doesn't match, build payment history automatically, sometimes earn rewards, and pay **zero** interest. Ever.
- **One of the most expensive consumer debts available** — a balance compounding at roughly 25% APR, where minimum payments keep it alive for a decade or more.

The habit that decides which product you own: **pay the statement balance in full, every month.** Do that and you occupy the grace period, where borrowed money costs nothing. Miss it once and you fall into the other product, where interest starts and the math turns hostile fast.

There's no third option and no in-between. That's why this is one of the highest-value single sentences in this course.

contentBlocks.title: Compounding, pointed at you
contentBlocks.content: Unit 2 taught you that compounding is what makes early investing so powerful. A carried card balance is that identical engine running in reverse, at a rate no reasonable investment reliably beats. Consider what that implies: paying off a 25% APR balance is effectively a **guaranteed, tax-free 25% return** — better than almost any investment you'll ever be offered, and it's available right now with no risk. This is why Unit 4 will tell you to kill high-APR debt before investing beyond the employer match: the 'safe' option here is the highest-return one.

## The folklore that costs the most

The single most expensive myth in consumer credit: *'you should carry a small balance to build your score.'*

It's false, and it's worth understanding exactly why. Scoring models see two things relevant here: whether you paid **on time**, and what **utilization** got reported. Paying your statement balance in full does both perfectly — the account reports activity and a paid-on-time history, and your utilization stays low.

Carrying a balance adds precisely one thing to that picture: **interest charges**. The score is identical. You are paying real money for zero points.

Where the myth comes from is a real-but-different fact: an account with *no activity at all* eventually contributes little (and may be closed by the issuer). Using the card and paying in full solves that completely. Activity ≠ carrying debt.

rows: Uses card, pays statement in full
Uses card, pays only minimum on $3,000
contentBlocks.caption: Rows one and two build credit identically. The only difference is the money in the interest column. That column is the entire cost of the myth.

interactionSequence.prompt: Two people have identical incomes and identical cards. One pays the statement in full monthly; the other carries $500 'for the score.' After three years, predict: how do their credit scores compare, and how much has the second person paid for the difference?

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: Their scores are essentially the same — both show perfect payment history and low utilization, which is what the model is looking at. The difference is roughly $375+ in interest over three years, paid by the second person, for nothing. This is the cleanest possible demonstration that the score system and the interest system are separate machines: satisfying the first one costs zero, and the myth quietly bills you for a benefit that was already free.

interactionSequence.prompt: You've been making on-time minimum payments on a $4,000 balance at 24% APR for two years and your score is good. What's the most accurate assessment?
options: You're handling this well — on-time payments are what matter, and your score proves it
Your score is fine, but that's a separate system from your wallet: interest has been compounding against you the whole time and the balance has barely moved
The minimum payment is designed to retire the balance efficiently, so you're on track
Once you've paid the original $4,000 in payments, the debt clears

optionFeedback.feedback: The on-time payments do protect the score — that's exactly the trap. The score being fine says nothing about the interest quietly draining you.
Correct — two separate systems. The score is healthy and the finances are bleeding, simultaneously.
Minimums are calculated to keep debt alive for years, not to retire it efficiently — most of each early payment is interest.
Interest never stops accruing on a carried balance; there's no point at which the original principal 'counts' as satisfied.

interactionSequence.prompt: A friend insists on carrying a small balance 'because the card company needs to see you using credit.' In your own words, give the two-sentence correction — including what their belief gets *almost* right.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The generous, accurate correction: they're right that an unused card goes stale — activity does matter. They're wrong that activity requires *debt*. Using the card and paying the statement in full reports exactly the same on-time history and low utilization, at zero interest. The whole myth is a confusion between *using* credit and *carrying* credit, and it's worth being kind about, because the intuition underneath it isn't stupid — it's just billing them for something free.

transferExercises.scenario: In Unit 2 you worked through Alex and Blake, where 7% compounding over decades turned modest early contributions into a large balance. A credit card runs the same engine at roughly 25%, in the opposite direction.
transferExercises.task: Write two or three sentences using the Unit 2 compounding logic to explain why paying off a 25% APR balance is a better 'investment' than putting the same money into an index fund expecting ~7% — and name the one exception this course will insist on.
successCriteria: Frames debt payoff as a guaranteed return equal to the APR, and compares it directly against expected market returns
Notes that the debt payoff return is guaranteed and risk-free, unlike market returns
Names the exception: capture a full employer match first, since a 100% match beats even a 25% APR

retrievalPractice.prompt: What single habit makes a credit card a free tool rather than an expensive debt, and what exactly does carrying a balance buy you in score terms?
Explain why paying off a 24% APR balance can be described as a guaranteed 24% return, and why that reframe changes where your next $500 should go.

learningExperiences.title: Not All Debt Is Equal: Building a Payoff Order
desiredCognitiveActions: Rank debts by true annual cost rather than emotional weight or balance size
Compare avalanche and snowball strategies on their actual trade-off
Sequence an employer match against debt payoff

contentBlocks.content:
## 'All debt is bad' is a feeling, not a plan

The instinct to treat every debt as equally urgent feels responsible. It's actually expensive, because it spreads your money evenly across debts that cost wildly different amounts.

Interest rate is the number that matters. A 25% APR credit card and a 5% student loan are not the same species of problem: the card is a fire, the loan is a bill. Every dollar has to go where it does the most work, and 'most work' means *kills the most expensive interest*.

So a payoff plan is a **ranking problem**, and the ranking is by rate — with one important thing that sits above the entire list.

contentBlocks.title: The employer match outranks everything
contentBlocks.content: If your employer matches 401(k) contributions, that match is a **guaranteed 50–100% return** the moment you contribute. Nothing on your debt list charges 100%. So the sequence is: **capture the full match first**, then attack high-APR debt, then everything else. Skipping the match to pay down a 6% loan is trading a 100% return for a 6% one — the most common expensive mistake in this whole area. (Unit 4 covers matches in detail; the sequencing point belongs here, where the money is being allocated.)

## Avalanche vs. snowball

Once the match is captured and minimums are covered everywhere, your surplus goes at *one* debt. Two defensible ways to pick it:

- **Avalanche** — highest interest rate first. Mathematically optimal: it minimizes total interest paid, always. This is the correct default.
- **Snowball** — smallest balance first. Mathematically worse, sometimes behaviorally better: knocking out a whole debt early produces a visible win and momentum.

The honest framing is a trade-off, not a right answer. Avalanche saves money; snowball can save the *plan*. If you'll stick with avalanche, use avalanche — it's strictly better on the numbers. If you've abandoned three payoff plans and the psychology is the real constraint, the plan you finish beats the plan you quit — which is a Unit 0 systems argument, not a math one.

What's *not* defensible is spreading extra money evenly across everything. That's not a strategy; it's the absence of one.

terms.definition: Paying minimums on everything, then directing all surplus at the highest-APR debt first. Minimizes total interest paid — the mathematically optimal order.
Paying minimums on everything, then directing all surplus at the smallest balance first. Costs more in interest than avalanche, but delivers early psychological wins.
Moving a card balance to a card offering a temporary low or 0% promotional rate, usually for a fee; helpful only with a concrete plan to clear it before the promo ends.

interactionSequence.prompt: You have $400/month above minimums. Your debts: Card A $5,000 at 26%, Card B $800 at 18%, student loan $20,000 at 5%. You also have an unclaimed employer match worth $200/month. Where does the money go?
options: All $400 at the student loan — it's the biggest balance and the most stressful
$200 to capture the full match, then the remaining $200 at Card A (26%)
Split it evenly — $133 at each debt so everything shrinks together
All $400 at Card A; the match can wait until the cards are gone

optionFeedback.feedback: Biggest balance ≠ most expensive. The 5% loan is the cheapest debt on the list while a 26% card burns.
Correct — the match's guaranteed 100% return outranks even 26%, then avalanche logic sends the rest at Card A.
Even splitting treats a 26% fire and a 5% bill identically — this is the 'all debt is equal' instinct, and it's the costliest option here.
Close, but the match forfeits permanently each year you skip it — a 100% return beats a 26% one, so it goes first.

interactionSequence.prompt: In your own words: when is the snowball method the *right* choice even though avalanche saves more money? Connect your answer to a Unit 0 idea.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The Unit 0 connection is that the optimal plan you abandon loses to the good plan you finish — the same logic that says two sustainable gym sessions beat a perfect program you quit. Snowball earns its interest cost when the binding constraint is motivation rather than math: an early, complete payoff is a visible win that keeps the system running. The honest caveat: this is a real trade-off with a real price, so it's worth naming the extra interest as the fee you're paying for momentum — not pretending snowball is free.

workedExamples.problem: Dana has $350/month above minimums, no employer match available, and three debts: Card A $6,000 at 25%, Card B $1,200 at 20%, car loan $9,000 at 6%. Build the payoff plan.
steps: **Cover all minimums first, always.** Missing any minimum damages payment history — the biggest score factor — and can trigger penalty APRs. Minimums are non-negotiable before any strategy runs.
**Check for a match.** None available here, so the top of the priority list is vacant and the surplus goes to debt. (If one existed, it would take the first $X before any of this.)
**Rank by rate, not balance.** Card A at 25%, Card B at 20%, car loan at 6%. Note the car loan has the *second-biggest balance* and is the *cheapest* debt — balance size is a distraction.
**Send all $350 at Card A.** Avalanche: everything else gets minimums only. Attacking the 25% debt saves more interest per dollar than any other target, so concentration beats spreading.

**Cascade on payoff.** When Card A clears, the entire $350 *plus* Card A's old minimum rolls onto Card B. When B clears, all of it rolls onto the car loan. The payment amount never shrinks — it just retargets, which is what makes the later payoffs accelerate.
**Leave the 6% loan at minimums until the cards are dead.** Prepaying a 6% loan while a 25% card burns is a guaranteed loss of about 19 percentage points per dollar.
workedExamples.conclusion: Dana's plan is one sentence: minimums everywhere, everything else at Card A, then cascade. Notice what it *doesn't* do — it doesn't touch the biggest balance first, doesn't spread money to feel balanced, and doesn't treat the car loan as urgent. The discipline is concentration: one target at a time, chosen by rate, with the freed-up payments rolling forward. If Dana had abandoned three plans before, snowball (Card B first, for the quick win) would be a defensible swap — paying a known interest premium for a system she'll actually finish.

fadedVariant.problem: Ravi has $600/month above minimums and an employer match worth $250/month that he currently isn't capturing. Debts: Card A $3,000 at 22%, medical bill $2,000 at 0% on a payment plan, student loan $30,000 at 4.5%. Build his plan.
givenSteps: Cover every minimum, including the medical payment plan — a 0% plan is still an obligation, and defaulting can send it to collections, which damages the report badly.
Capture the full $250 match before any debt payoff: a 100% guaranteed return outranks the 22% card.
blankedStepPrompts: Where do the remaining $350/month go, and how should the 0% medical bill be treated in the ranking?
Why does the $30,000 student loan — by far the largest balance — stay at minimums, and what would change that?
blankedStepAnswers: The remaining $350 goes at Card A (22%) — the highest-rate debt. The 0% medical bill is genuinely free money: it gets its scheduled minimum and nothing more, because there's no interest cost to eliminate. Paying it early accomplishes nothing financially; it just feels tidy. Rank by rate, and 0% ranks last by definition.

The student loan stays at minimums because 4.5% is the cheapest debt on the list — every dollar sent there instead of the 22% card loses about 17.5 percentage points. Balance size is irrelevant to the ranking; only rate matters. What would change it: the cards being cleared (then the surplus cascades to the loan), or the loan's rate being variable and rising above the alternatives.

transferExercises.scenario: In Unit 2 you found your real surplus by sorting a month of statements. That number is exactly the input a payoff plan needs — a plan built on a fantasy surplus fails in week three.
transferExercises.task: Using your actual surplus figure from Unit 2, build your own payoff order: list every debt with its rate, note any employer match, and state where your next surplus dollar goes and why. If you have no debt, do it for the emergency fund and investing instead — the ranking logic is the same.
successCriteria: Uses the real Unit 2 surplus number, not an aspirational one
Debts are ranked by rate with the match (if any) placed above the entire list
A single, specific next target is named — not an even split — with the strategy (avalanche or snowball) stated explicitly

retrievalPractice.prompt: State the full priority order for a surplus dollar when you have debt: what comes first, second, and third, and what's the ranking criterion?
Contrast avalanche and snowball: what does each optimize for, what does each cost, and what determines which one a given person should use?

learningExperiences.title: The 15-Minute Contract Skim
desiredCognitiveActions: Locate the five load-bearing clause types in a real agreement
Assess which party each clause favors
Distinguish boilerplate from the terms that create asymmetric risk

contentBlocks.content:
## Nobody reads contracts. Read five clauses instead.

The advice 'always read the contract' is useless, because nobody reads 30 pages of legalese for a gym membership, and telling people they should just makes them feel guilty while changing nothing. It's a willpower answer to a systems problem.

Here's the systems answer: **you don't read the contract, you hunt it.** Almost every clause in a consumer agreement is genuinely boilerplate. A small number are *load-bearing* — they're the ones that will ever actually cost you money or trap you. Find those five, read only those, and you've captured most of the value in about 15 minutes.

And the stakes are real: courts generally enforce what you signed, whether or not you read it. 'I didn't know that was in there' is not a defense — which is exactly why the hunt is worth learning.

## The five clauses to hunt

Search the document (Ctrl+F is a legitimate contract-reading technique) for these:

1. **Auto-renewal** — does this extend itself automatically, and what's the cancellation *window*? The classic trap: a 12-month term that renews unless cancelled 30–60 days before it ends, at a price you never re-agreed to. Search: *renew, term, notice*.
2. **Termination & fees** — what does leaving cost? Early-termination fees, restocking fees, 'liquidated damages.' Search: *terminate, cancel, fee, penalty*.
3. **Rate/price changes** — can they change what you pay unilaterally? Variable rates, 'we may modify pricing with notice,' promotional rates that expire into much higher ones. Search: *rate, change, modify, promotional*.
4. **Dispute resolution** — arbitration clauses, class-action waivers, and which state's law governs. These decide what you can do if things go wrong. Search: *arbitration, dispute, waive, jurisdiction*.
5. **Default & what triggers it** — what counts as breaking the deal, and what happens then? Acceleration clauses, penalty APRs, collections. Search: *default, breach, accelerate*.

Each one answers a single question: **what does this cost me, and when can they change it?**

contentBlocks.title: Asymmetry is the tell
contentBlocks.content: You don't need legal training to evaluate a clause. Ask one question: **does this bind both parties, or just me?** A term that lets *them* change the price with notice but locks *you* in for 12 months is asymmetric. A cancellation window that's 60 days for you and instant for them is asymmetric. Asymmetry isn't automatically predatory — the drafter always writes in their own favor — but it tells you precisely where the risk sits, and which terms are worth trying to negotiate.

interactionSequence.prompt: A gym contract: 12-month term, $40/month, 'automatically renews for successive 12-month terms unless written notice is provided no fewer than 45 days prior to the end of the term.' What's the practical risk?
options: Minimal — you can cancel any time you stop wanting the gym
You must remember to cancel in a narrow window ~10 months from now, or you're bound to another full year — set a calendar reminder now
Auto-renewal clauses are unenforceable, so it doesn't matter
The risk is only the $40/month; it's a small amount

optionFeedback.feedback: The term binds you for 12 months, and the renewal binds you for another 12 unless you act in the window — 'stop wanting it' isn't a cancellation method.
Correct — and the calendar reminder is the systems fix: the clause is designed to beat memory, so don't rely on memory.
Auto-renewal is regulated in some places but broadly enforceable; assuming otherwise is an expensive bet.
The risk is $480 — a full unwanted year — not $40. The clause converts a small monthly decision into a large annual one.

interactionSequence.prompt: In your own words: why is 'contracts are just boilerplate, nothing in them is negotiable or worth reading' both understandable and expensive?

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: Understandable: most of the document genuinely is boilerplate, and reading all of it is a bad use of 30 minutes — so the instinct to skip is a rational response to a badly designed artifact. Expensive: the small number of load-bearing clauses vary enormously between contracts, they're frequently negotiable (especially fees, terms, and renewal windows), and courts enforce them regardless of whether you read them. The fix isn't 'read more' — it's 'read *selectively*': hunt five clauses, skip the rest, and negotiate the ones that bind you asymmetrically.

workedExamples.problem: You're signing up for a $70/month internet plan. The agreement is 22 pages. Run the 15-minute skim.
steps: **Hunt auto-renewal and term.** Search *term, renew*. Finding: 24-month agreement, then month-to-month. Risk: you're locked for two years, so the price and service quality matter more than a one-month trial would suggest.

**Hunt termination and fees.** Search *cancel, fee, terminate*. Finding: early-termination fee of $15 × months remaining. Risk: cancelling at month six costs $270. This is the clause that decides whether 'I'll just switch if it's bad' is actually available to you — usually it isn't.

**Hunt price changes.** Search *promotional, rate, modify*. Finding: $70 is a 12-month promotional rate; it reverts to $95 for months 13–24, and the provider may adjust fees with notice. Risk: your real average price is about $82.50/month, not $70, and you're locked in for the expensive half.

**Hunt dispute resolution.** Search *arbitration, class*. Finding: binding arbitration, class-action waiver. Risk: if something goes systematically wrong, your remedies are individual and limited. Common and usually non-negotiable — but now you know.
**Hunt default.** Search *default, suspend*. Finding: late payment permits service suspension plus a reconnection fee. Risk: a missed payment is more expensive than it looks.

**Apply the asymmetry test and act.** They can raise fees with notice; you owe $270 to leave. That asymmetry is the deal's shape. Concrete actions: calendar the month-12 price jump *now*, calendar the month-24 contract end, and try one negotiation — ask them to waive the installation fee or extend the promo rate, since these are frequently granted to anyone who asks.
workedExamples.conclusion: Fifteen minutes, five searches, and the plan you thought cost $70 costs an average of $82.50 with a $270 exit toll. You may still sign it — that's fine, this is about signing *knowingly*. Note the two outputs that make it real: calendar reminders (the systems fix for clauses designed to beat memory) and one negotiation attempt. Unit 5 gives you the negotiation structure; Unit 7 points this same skim at a lease, where the stakes are ten times higher.

fadedVariant.problem: A 'free' 30-day trial of a software service asks for your card. The terms are 12 pages. Run the skim.
givenSteps: Hunt auto-renewal first — for trials, this is always the load-bearing clause. Finding: the trial converts to a $29/month subscription automatically at day 31 unless cancelled, and the card is charged without further notice.
Apply the asymmetry test: they get an automatic charge; you get a window that requires you to remember an obligation 30 days out.
blankedStepPrompts: What's the systems-over-willpower fix here, and why is it more reliable than intending to cancel?
Which of the other four clause types would you still check for a low-price subscription, and why does one of them matter more than the dollar amount suggests?
blankedStepAnswers: Set a calendar reminder for day 25 the moment you sign up — before you close the tab. The whole business model of free trials depends on your memory failing at exactly the right moment for them; the reminder converts a willpower problem into a solved system, the same move as the payday auto-transfer in Unit 2. Intending to cancel is precisely the strategy the clause is priced against.

Termination/cancellation is the one that punches above the price: check *how* you're allowed to cancel. Some services accept one click; others require a phone call during business hours or written notice — friction deliberately added to make cancelling harder than subscribing. That asymmetry, not the $29, is what turns a cheap subscription into a year of charges you meant to stop.

transferExercises.scenario: Unit 7 applies contract skills to a lease — the largest contract most young adults sign, where a single clause can cost thousands. The skim you just learned is the same procedure at ten times the stakes.
transferExercises.task: Take an agreement you're *currently* party to (gym, phone, streaming, insurance, or your actual lease) and run the five-clause hunt on it. Write down what you find for each of the five, and one thing you'd try to negotiate or one calendar reminder you should set today.
successCriteria: All five clause types are searched for, with a finding or an explicit 'not present' for each
At least one asymmetric clause is identified and named as favoring the other party
The output is an action — a calendar reminder set, or a specific negotiation ask — not just a summary

retrievalPractice.prompt: Name the five load-bearing clause types to hunt in any consumer contract, and the single question each one answers.
What's the 'asymmetry test' for evaluating a clause without legal training, and why is a promotional rate that expires into a higher one worth catching before you sign?

sections.title: Unit 4 — Growing & Protecting Wealth
sections.description: The offensive half of personal finance, plus the shield that keeps it from being knocked over: capturing an employer match and starting retirement accounts while time is on your side, running the highest-leverage negotiation of your life, understanding why a raise can never lower your take-home pay, and choosing insurance by total cost rather than premium. This unit follows Credit & Debt deliberately — high-interest debt outruns almost any investment — and it cashes in the compounding reasoning built in Unit 2.

learningObjectives.description: Compute the dollar value of an employer match, choose between Roth and traditional for a stated income scenario, and sequence the standard contribution priority order.
Draft a complete counter-offer — researched range, warm framing, specific ask — and plan for the three most likely responses.
Compute tax owed from a bracket table, distinguish marginal from effective rate, and evaluate a raise's true after-tax value.
Compare two insurance plans on total annual cost under low-use and high-use scenarios, and recommend one with justification.

motivationHooks.prompt: Two numbers to guess before this unit. (1) You decline to negotiate a first offer, accepting $72,000 instead of the $78,000 you could have had. Over a 40-year career with raises compounding off that base, roughly what does that one silent moment cost? (2) Your employer matches 4% of a $60,000 salary and you contribute nothing. What do you forfeit this year? Write both down.

This unit contains the two highest-dollar-per-minute actions in the entire course: a five-minute conversation that can be worth six figures over a career, and a twenty-minute account setup that captures thousands of dollars a year in free money. Almost nobody does either at 25. The people who do aren't smarter — they just knew these were on the table.

Both answers land higher than people expect. The $6,000 gap isn't $6,000 — because raises are usually percentages of your base and future employers ask what you make now, the gap compounds through every subsequent raise and job change, running well into six figures over a career. And the unclaimed match is $2,400 forfeited *this year alone* — a guaranteed 100% return declined, which then doesn't compound for the next 40 years either. Both are things you get by asking, once.

sectionSummary.content: You should now believe four things. **First**, the employer match is not a retirement perk — it's unpaid compensation you forfeit every year you don't claim it, at a guaranteed 50–100% return that outranks every debt and investment on your list; capture it, open a Roth IRA, buy broad index funds, and stop touching it. **Second**, salary negotiation is the highest-leverage conversation of your career because raises compound off your base: research a range, anchor warmly above your target, make one specific ask, then stop talking and let the silence do its work. **Third**, tax brackets are *marginal* — only the dollars above each threshold pay the higher rate, so a raise can never lower your take-home pay, and your effective rate is always below your marginal rate. **Fourth**, the cheapest insurance plan is not the one with the lowest premium: total annual cost is premium plus expected cost-sharing, and the right choice depends on realistic usage, which is why you run the math under both a low-use and a high-use year.
keyTakeaways: Capture the full employer match first — a guaranteed 50–100% return beats every alternative
Counter every offer: warm framing, researched range, one specific number, then silence
Brackets are marginal — a raise always increases take-home pay; effective rate < marginal rate
Compare plans on premium + expected cost-sharing under both low-use and high-use years

furtherReading.title: The Simple Path to Wealth
furtherReading.citation: Collins, J. L. (2016). The Simple Path to Wealth: Your Road Map to Financial Independence and a Rich, Free Life.
furtherReading.author: J. L. Collins
furtherReading.rationaleNote: The clearest popular case for the boring default this unit recommends: capture the match, buy broad low-cost index funds, ignore the noise for decades.

furtherReading.title: Bogleheads Wiki — Investment Start-Up Kit
furtherReading.citation: Bogleheads. Getting started.
furtherReading.rationaleNote: A free, non-commercial community reference on account types, contribution priority order, and index investing — no product being sold, which is rare in this space.

furtherReading.title: Never Split the Difference
furtherReading.citation: Voss, C. (2016). Never Split the Difference: Negotiating As If Your Life Depended On It. Harper Business.
furtherReading.author: Chris Voss
furtherReading.rationaleNote: Strong on the tactical psychology this experience only touches — calibrated questions, tactical silence, and staying warm while holding a number.

furtherReading.title: IRS — Tax Withholding Estimator and Bracket Information
furtherReading.citation: Internal Revenue Service. Tax withholding estimator and current bracket tables.
furtherReading.author: Internal Revenue Service
furtherReading.rationaleNote: The authoritative source for current-year brackets, standard deduction, and contribution limits — all of which change annually, so check here rather than trusting any static figure.

furtherReading.title: HealthCare.gov — Glossary and Plan Comparison Tools
furtherReading.citation: HealthCare.gov. Glossary of health coverage terms.
furtherReading.rationaleNote: Plain-language official definitions of deductible, coinsurance, and out-of-pocket maximum, plus how the pieces interact — useful next to any employer's benefits packet.

learningExperiences.title: Free Money First: The Match, the Accounts, the Order
desiredCognitiveActions: Compute the dollar value of an employer match and recognize it as forfeited compensation
Choose Roth vs. traditional using current-versus-future tax-rate reasoning
Sequence the standard contribution priority order against debt and cash goals

contentBlocks.content:
## The match is not a perk. It's your money.

If your employer offers a 401(k) match, the correct mental model is not 'a retirement benefit I'll get around to.' It's: **part of your salary is sitting unclaimed, and it expires annually.**

Run it. A 100% match up to 4% of a $60,000 salary means: contribute $2,400, receive $2,400. That's an instant, guaranteed 100% return — before the market does anything at all. There is no investment on earth that reliably offers that. It beats paying off a 25% credit card. It beats everything in this course.

And it doesn't roll over. Every year you don't contribute, that $2,400 is simply gone — and then Unit 2's compounding math compounds the loss: that forfeited $2,400 also doesn't grow for the next 40 years.

So the first move in this entire unit takes about twenty minutes: find out whether you have a match, and contribute at least enough to capture all of it.

contentBlocks.title: Check your vesting schedule
contentBlocks.content: Matched money often **vests** over time — meaning it becomes fully yours only after a set period (say, three years), and leaving early can forfeit some of it. This doesn't change the advice (capture the match regardless — partial free money is still free money), but it's worth knowing before you job-hop, and it's a genuine, quantifiable item in a job-change decision. Your own contributions are always 100% yours immediately; only the *employer's* portion vests.

## Roth vs. traditional, in one question

The two account flavors differ on *when* you pay tax:

- **Traditional** — contribute pre-tax (lowering this year's taxable income), pay tax on withdrawals in retirement.
- **Roth** — contribute after-tax (no break now), withdrawals in retirement are tax-free.

Strip away the noise and it's one question: **is your tax rate higher now, or will it be higher when you withdraw?** Pay the tax when your rate is lower.

For most people in their twenties, the answer leans **Roth**: you're likely near the lowest earning point of your career, so paying tax now at a low rate to make decades of growth tax-free is usually a good trade. A high earner already in a top bracket has a stronger case for traditional's up-front break. Nobody knows future tax law, which is a real argument for holding some of each over a career.

The accounts themselves: a **401(k)** comes through your employer (that's where the match lives); an **IRA** you open yourself at any brokerage, in about twenty minutes, with either flavor.

## The priority order

This is the sequence that resolves every 'where does my next dollar go?' question in this course — and it's ordered by guaranteed return:

1. **A small starter emergency buffer** (Unit 2) — so an emergency doesn't send you back to a 25% card.
2. **The full employer match** — a guaranteed 50–100%. Nothing beats it.
3. **High-interest debt** (Unit 3) — a guaranteed return equal to the APR. A 25% card beats any expected market return.
4. **The rest of the emergency fund** — 3–6 months of essentials.
5. **Max out tax-advantaged space** — Roth IRA, more 401(k).
6. **Low-interest debt and taxable investing** — the 4% student loan finally gets attention.

Notice what's doing the work: the list is sorted by *certainty and size of return*, which is why a guaranteed 100% match sits above a 25% card, which sits above a hoped-for 7% market.

And what to actually buy inside the account? For almost everyone starting out: **a broad, low-cost index fund**, then leave it alone. Contribution limits change annually — check the IRS for the current year rather than trusting any number you read once.

terms.definition: The timeline over which employer-contributed money becomes fully yours; your own contributions vest immediately.
The maximum you may put into a tax-advantaged account in a year, set by the IRS and adjusted annually.
The annual percentage a fund charges to manage your money; small differences compound enormously over decades, which is why low-cost index funds are the default recommendation.
An account (401(k), IRA, HSA) receiving special tax treatment to encourage long-term saving, in exchange for rules about withdrawals.

interactionSequence.prompt: You earn $70,000. Your employer matches 50% of contributions up to 6% of salary. You contribute nothing. What are you forfeiting annually?
options: $0 — the match only has value at retirement
$2,100 — 50% of the 6% ($4,200) you'd need to contribute
$4,200 — the full 6% of salary
$700 — 1% of salary

optionFeedback.feedback: It's compensation forfeited *this year* — and then it also never compounds, which doubles the loss.
Correct — contribute $4,200 (6%), receive $2,100. A guaranteed 50% return on your contribution.
That's what *you'd* contribute; the match is 50% of it.
Recompute: 6% of $70,000 is $4,200, and the employer matches half of that.

interactionSequence.prompt: You have $500/month of surplus, a 100% employer match available up to $200/month, a $4,000 card balance at 24%, a $25,000 student loan at 4%, and no emergency fund. Order these from *first* to *last* priority.
options: Contribute $200/month to capture the full employer match
Attack the 24% credit card balance
Build out the full 3–6 month emergency fund
Make extra payments on the 4% student loan

feedback.correctiveFeedback: Sorted by guaranteed return: the match is 100% (nothing beats it), the card is a guaranteed 24%, the emergency fund is insurance against being forced back onto that card, and the 4% loan is the cheapest money on the list — it stays at minimums until everything above is handled. One nuance the ordering hides: a *small* starter buffer (a few hundred to $1,000) is worth carving out before or alongside the card attack, so the first flat tire doesn't undo your progress. Full fund after the card; starter buffer before.
hints: Sort by the *certainty and size* of the return, not by which debt feels most stressful.
One item here offers a guaranteed 100% return — that's not a number any debt or market can match.

confidencePrompt.prompt: How confident are you in this ordering?

interactionSequence.prompt: In your own words: explain to a 24-year-old earning $45,000 why Roth is probably the right choice for them — and name the situation where you'd say the opposite.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The reasoning to land: at $45,000 they're likely near the lowest tax rate of their career, so paying tax *now* to make forty years of growth tax-free is buying at a discount — and Unit 2's compounding says those decades of growth are where nearly all the money is. The flip: someone already earning in a top bracket gets a large, immediate deduction from traditional, and may well withdraw at a lower rate in retirement — so the trade reverses. The honest caveat worth including: nobody knows future tax law, which is a legitimate argument for holding some of each over a career rather than betting everything on one prediction.

workedExamples.problem: Nia, 26, earns $65,000. Her employer matches 100% up to 5%. She has a $2,000 card balance at 22%, a $1,000 starter buffer, and $600/month of surplus. She's never contributed to any retirement account. Build her plan.
steps: **Compute the match.** 5% of $65,000 = $3,250/year, or about $271/month. Contributing $271 gets her $271 — a guaranteed 100% return. This is the single highest-return use of a dollar available to her, so it goes first.

**Set the 401(k) contribution to 5% today.** Not next month. Every month she waits forfeits about $271 permanently. This is a fifteen-minute HR portal task, and the Unit 0 move applies: shrink it to 'open the benefits portal and find the contribution field.'
**Aim the remaining $329/month at the 22% card.** A guaranteed 22% return, second only to the match. The $2,000 balance clears in roughly six months, at which point the whole $329 is freed.
**Keep the $1,000 buffer intact meanwhile.** It's what stops a car repair from putting the card right back where it was — protecting the payoff plan is part of the payoff plan.

**On card payoff, cascade.** The freed $329 now goes to finishing the 3–6 month emergency fund (Unit 2 sizing: 3–6 × her essential expenses), then to opening and funding a **Roth IRA** — at 26 and $65,000, paying tax now at a likely-low rate to get decades of tax-free growth is the standard call.

**Buy the boring thing.** Inside both accounts: a broad, low-cost index fund. Then don't touch it. Check the current year's IRS contribution limits when she funds the Roth, since they change annually.
workedExamples.conclusion: Nia's whole plan is a priority list executed in order: match now, card next, fund after, Roth after that — sorted by guaranteed return, not by what feels urgent. The two moves that matter most are the two she can do this week: set the 5% contribution (recovering $3,250/year she's currently declining) and point the rest at the 22% card. Note how Unit 2's compounding and Unit 3's payoff ranking are doing all the work here — this unit is where those two threads get spent.

fadedVariant.problem: Theo, 29, earns $110,000 as a contractor — no employer, no 401(k), no match. He has no debt, a full emergency fund, and $1,200/month of surplus. What changes?
givenSteps: Recognize what's absent: with no employer there's no match to capture, so the top of the priority list is vacant and the highest-return rung simply doesn't exist for him. With no high-interest debt and a full fund, rungs one through four are all satisfied.
That puts him straight at 'max out tax-advantaged space' — but the *accounts available* to him differ from an employee's, since a workplace 401(k) isn't on the table.
blankedStepPrompts: What tax-advantaged options does a self-employed person still have, and how would you find the current rules rather than guessing?
Does the Roth-vs-traditional reasoning change for Theo at $110,000, and what's the honest answer about certainty here?
blankedStepAnswers: He still has an IRA (Roth or traditional, subject to income rules), and self-employed people have access to dedicated vehicles like a SEP-IRA or a solo 401(k) that allow substantially larger contributions than an IRA alone. The critical move is *not* to trust any specific figure from memory: contribution limits, income phase-outs, and eligibility rules change annually, so check the IRS directly or consult a tax professional — this is exactly the kind of detail where a stale number costs real money.

The reasoning is the same question — is my rate higher now or later? — but the answer genuinely gets harder at $110,000. He's in a higher bracket now, which strengthens the case for traditional's up-front deduction, and Roth IRA eligibility begins to phase out at higher incomes anyway. The honest answer is that this is where the simple heuristic runs out: at his income, with self-employment complexity, the reasoning supports splitting across both flavors or getting one session with a tax professional. Recognizing where a rule of thumb stops applying is itself the skill.

transferExercises.scenario: In Unit 2 you ran the Alex/Blake comparison and saw that early dollars dominate. The match is that principle with a multiplier bolted on: matched dollars are doubled *before* they start compounding.
transferExercises.task: Using a compound-interest calculator, run your own match scenario: take a plausible annual match amount (or $2,000 if you have none yet), and compute what capturing it every year from your current age to 65 becomes at ~7%. Then compute what starting that same habit ten years from now becomes. Write down both numbers and the gap.
successCriteria: Uses a realistic match figure based on an actual or plausible employer plan
Isolates start date as the only variable between the two runs
States the dollar gap and connects it explicitly to the 'the match expires annually' point — the loss is the forfeited match *plus* its forgone compounding

selfGuidedExercise.objective: Find out whether you're leaving match money on the table, and capture it.
selfGuidedExercise.realWorldContext: Your actual employer benefits portal, or your current account situation if self-employed or between jobs.
instructions: Open your employer's benefits portal (or your offer letter / HR documentation) and find the 401(k) match terms: what percentage, up to what limit?
Compute the annual dollar value of the full match on your actual salary. Write the number down — this is what you're currently earning or forfeiting.
Check your current contribution rate. If it's below the match threshold, raise it to at least capture the full match today, in this session.
Find your vesting schedule and note it — it's a real input to any future job-change decision.

If you have no employer match (self-employed, contractor, between jobs): spend the same 25 minutes identifying which tax-advantaged accounts you're eligible for, checking the IRS site for current-year limits.
materialsOrPrerequisites: Access to your benefits portal or HR documentation
reflectionPrompts: What was the annual dollar value of your full match — and how does it compare to what you'd guessed before looking?
If you weren't capturing it fully, how many years has that been true, and what's the cumulative forfeited amount?
What friction stopped you from doing this earlier, and what Unit 0 move would have removed it?

selfAssessmentCriteria: The actual match terms were located in real documentation, not assumed
The annual dollar value was computed from real salary
The contribution rate was actually changed if it was below the match threshold

retrievalPractice.prompt: State the full contribution priority order from first dollar to last, and name the principle the ordering is sorted by.
Roth vs. traditional reduces to one question — what is it, and which way does it usually resolve for someone in their twenties?

learningExperiences.title: The Five-Minute Conversation Worth Six Figures
desiredCognitiveActions: Reason about why base salary compounds through a career
Construct a warm, data-anchored counter-offer
Plan responses to the three likely replies, including a flat no

contentBlocks.content:
## Why this one conversation compounds

A $6,000 gap in a starting salary is not $6,000. Here's why it multiplies:

- **Raises are percentages of your base.** A 3% raise on $78,000 is bigger than a 3% raise on $72,000, forever, and the gap widens every year.
- **Your next offer anchors on this one.** Future employers ask about — or benchmark against — your current compensation. The gap follows you across jobs.
- **Your match follows your base.** A percentage match on a larger salary is more free money, which then compounds per Unit 2.

Stack those and a single $6,000 concession at 25 runs well into six figures over a career. Which makes the *ask itself* — five minutes of mild discomfort — arguably the highest hourly-value action available to you.

And yet most people don't do it. Not from ignorance, but from a specific fear worth addressing directly.

contentBlocks.title: "What if they rescind the offer?"
contentBlocks.content: This is the fear that costs the most, and it's wildly out of proportion to the risk. Hiring is expensive and slow: by the time an offer reaches you, an employer has invested heavily in choosing you, and a polite, data-backed counter is an entirely expected part of the process — recruiters negotiate constantly and are not surprised or offended by it. Rescissions over a reasonable counter are rare and usually signal an employer you're better off avoiding. The realistic downside of asking is that they say *no* and you accept the original offer — which is exactly where you'd have been anyway. Asymmetric bet: small, bounded downside; six-figure upside.

## The structure

A strong counter has exactly three components and one discipline:

1. **Warmth.** Open with genuine enthusiasm for the role. You're not an adversary; you're a person they already chose who wants to say yes. *"I'm really excited about this role and the team..."*
2. **External data.** Anchor on researched market rates, not your needs. *"Based on my research for this role in this market, the range is $78–88k..."* — the number comes from the market, not from wanting more, which makes it hard to argue with and impossible to take personally.
3. **One specific ask.** Name a number, at or slightly above your target. Vagueness ('is there any flexibility?') invites a vague answer. Specific asks get specific responses.

And the discipline: **then stop talking.**

Silence after an ask feels unbearable, and filling it is how people negotiate against themselves — *"...but of course I understand if that's not possible!"* undoes the entire ask. Let it sit. The discomfort is doing work: it's their turn, and the next voice should be theirs.

rows: Standard. The number was likely not final.
Thank them warmly. Don't negotiate against yourself while waiting.
You just made $4,000 in five minutes.
Accept, or make one small final ask (start date, PTO, review timing). Don't grind.
Often true (bands, equity, policy). Base is closed; other doors aren't.
Pivot: signing bonus, PTO, remote days, title, or a 6-month review with a raise target in writing.
"That's above our range for the level."
A title/level issue, not a you issue.
Ask what the range for the *next* level is and what it takes to get there — sets up your next negotiation.
contentBlocks.caption: Plan all four before the call. The one you least want — the flat no — is the one most worth rehearsing.

interactionSequence.prompt: You counter a $72,000 offer, asking for $80,000 based on researched market data, delivered warmly. Predict the distribution of outcomes across 100 people who do exactly this. How many get more money? How many get the offer rescinded?

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The realistic shape: a substantial share get *something* — a full or partial increase, a signing bonus, or a non-salary concession — because first offers frequently have room built in. Most of the rest get a polite 'the base is fixed,' after which the original offer still stands. Rescissions are rare enough to be a story people tell rather than a risk people encounter, and when they happen they've usually involved aggression or ultimatums, not a warm data-backed ask. If your prediction had rescission as a meaningful chunk, notice what that fear has been costing you: it's guarding against the rarest outcome by guaranteeing the worst one.

interactionSequence.prompt: You've just said: "Based on my research the range is $78–88k, so I was hoping we could get to $80,000." There's silence on the line. It's been four seconds. What do you do?
options: Fill the silence — add "but I'm flexible, and I understand if the budget's tight"
Say nothing and let the silence sit; it's their turn to speak
Lower the ask preemptively to seem reasonable
Explain in detail why you need more money — rent, loans, cost of living

optionFeedback.feedback: This is negotiating against yourself — you just conceded before they responded, and the ask evaporates.
Correct — the silence is uncomfortable by design and it's doing work. Let them answer.
Bidding against yourself with no counter-pressure. You've now anchored *below* your own ask.
Your expenses aren't their concern and can't be verified; market data is the anchor that works because it's external to both of you.

interactionSequence.prompt: In your own words: why does anchoring on *external market data* work better than explaining what you need or what you're worth? Connect it to the interests-vs-positions idea you'll meet in Unit 5.

feedback.elaborativeFeedback: The mechanism: market data makes the number *about the role*, not about you. That does three things — it's verifiable (they can check it), it's impersonal (declining it isn't a judgment of you, and pushing for it isn't greed), and it gives the hiring manager something to take to their own boss as justification. 'I need more for rent' is a position rooted in your circumstances, which they can't act on and can't verify; 'the market pays $78–88k for this' identifies a shared interest — neither party wants an under-market offer that leads to you leaving in a year. You've made it a joint problem about facts rather than a contest of wills.

workedExamples.problem: You're offered $72,000 for a role you want. Research says the market range is $78,000–$88,000. Build and deliver the counter.
steps: **Research first, so the anchor is real.** Check multiple sources — salary sites, levels databases, industry surveys, and ideally two people in similar roles. Vague confidence isn't an anchor; a defensible range is. Land on: $78–88k for this role, this market, this experience level.
**Pick the ask.** Target ~$80k; ask at or slightly above it, inside the researched range. Asking at the very top invites a fight; asking below your target guarantees you land under it.

**Never counter in the moment.** When the offer arrives: thank them warmly, express real enthusiasm, and ask for the details in writing plus a day or two. This is the Unit 0 emotional-regulation move — offers arrive with a spike (excitement, relief, fear), and spike-state is when people accept instantly and regret it.

**Draft the three sentences.** *"I'm really excited about this role — the team and the work are exactly what I'm looking for. Based on my research for this position in this market, the range is $78–88k, so I was hoping we could get closer to $80,000. Is that something we can do?"* Warmth, external data, one specific number.
**Deliver it, then stop talking.** Say it, and let the silence sit. Do not soften, explain, or hedge. The next voice should be theirs.
**Have all four responses pre-planned** (see the table above), especially the flat no — pivot to signing bonus, PTO, remote flexibility, title, or a written six-month review with a raise target.
**Get it in writing** before you resign anything anywhere.
workedExamples.conclusion: Total time: about five minutes of conversation on top of an hour of research. Expected value: frequently thousands immediately, compounding to six figures across a career. The three components — warmth, external data, one specific ask — are the whole technique, and the silence is what makes them work. Note how much of this is really Unit 0: shrink the task (draft three sentences), regulate the spike (never accept in the moment), and pre-write the reframe for a no so a single rejection doesn't retire the skill.

fadedVariant.problem: You've been at your job three years, never negotiated, and believe you're underpaid. There's no offer on the table — you're asking your current manager for a raise. Adapt the approach.
givenSteps: Recognize what's different: there's no offer deadline creating natural urgency, and your manager likely can't approve a raise unilaterally — they have to advocate for it upward, often on a budget cycle you don't control.
Research the same way: external market data for your role, level, and market — plus, this time, internal evidence of your contribution.
blankedStepPrompts: What replaces the offer's built-in leverage, and how should the timing of the ask change?
How does the 'warmth + external data + one specific ask' structure adapt when you're asking your own manager, and what are you actually asking them to do?
blankedStepAnswers: Your leverage becomes documented contribution plus market data — a record of what you've delivered, ideally quantified, against what the market pays for it. Timing matters far more than in an offer negotiation: ask *ahead* of the budget/review cycle, not after numbers are locked, and open the conversation weeks early rather than dropping it in a review. A credible outside offer is real leverage, but only bring one you'd actually accept — bluffing with a job you'd never take is how people end up quitting by accident.

The structure holds but the ask shifts. Warmth is genuine (you want to stay and grow here). External data still anchors the number. But the specific ask is often two-part: a number *and* a request for their advocacy — 'what would it take to get to $X, and can you help make the case?' You're not extracting money from your manager; you're recruiting them as your ally against a budget process. If the answer is no, the follow-up ask is the concrete path: what specifically, by when, gets me there — in writing.

transferExercises.scenario: In Unit 0 you pre-wrote the accurate reframe for 'my counter was declined flatly.' Now you have the actual skill it was protecting.
transferExercises.task: Revisit that reframe with what you now know. Rewrite it in three sentences: the fact, what the evidence actually supports about a fixed base, and the specific next action (which should now be a concrete non-base ask or a review-timing request, not a vague resolution).
successCriteria: The fact is stated neutrally in one sentence, with no three-P's story attached
The interpretation correctly identifies that a fixed base is usually about bands and budgets, not a judgment of the person
The next action is specific and drawn from this experience's response table — a named non-base ask or a written review target

retrievalPractice.prompt: Name the three components of a strong counter-offer and the one discipline that follows the ask. Why does each component work?
They say: "The base is fixed at $72k." Give three specific things you'd ask for instead, and explain why base being fixed doesn't close the negotiation.

learningExperiences.title: Brackets Are Marginal: Killing the Raise Myth
desiredCognitiveActions: Compute tax bracket-by-bracket rather than applying one rate to all income
Distinguish marginal from effective rate
Evaluate a raise's true after-tax value

contentBlocks.content:
## The myth that makes people refuse money

Someone, somewhere, has told you: *"Careful — that raise could push you into a higher bracket and you'd take home less."* People have declined raises, refused overtime, and turned down promotions over this.

It is false. Completely, mechanically false.

The U.S. income tax is **progressive and marginal**, which means income is taxed in *layers*. Each bracket's rate applies **only to the dollars inside that bracket** — never to your whole income. Crossing into a higher bracket means the *next* dollars are taxed more; every dollar below the line keeps its old, lower rate.

So a raise always increases your take-home pay. Always. The only question is by how much.

contentBlocks.title: Think of it as filling buckets
contentBlocks.content: Picture your income pouring into a stack of buckets. The first bucket fills at 10%, and once full, income spills into the next at 12%, then 22%, and so on. Nothing that's already settled in the 10% bucket gets re-taxed when the 22% bucket starts filling. A raise just means a bit more water in a higher bucket — you never lose what's below. This is why your **effective rate** (total tax ÷ total income) is *always lower* than your **marginal rate** (the rate on your next dollar): the blended average of all your buckets is dragged down by the cheap ones underneath.

## The rest of the machinery, briefly

Four more pieces worth knowing:

- **Standard deduction** — an amount subtracted from your income before any tax applies. Most people take it rather than itemizing, meaning your first chunk of income is effectively taxed at 0%.
- **Withholding** — tax your employer removes from each paycheck as an *estimate* of your year's bill. Filing reconciles the estimate against reality.
- **Refund ≠ winning.** A big refund means you over-withheld — you gave the government an interest-free loan all year and got your own money back. The ideal is accurate withholding, with the difference working for you (per Unit 2's compounding) instead of sitting at the IRS.
- **Credits beat deductions.** A *deduction* reduces taxable income (worth your marginal rate). A *credit* reduces tax owed dollar-for-dollar. A $1,000 credit is worth $1,000; a $1,000 deduction might be worth $220.

One honest note: brackets, the standard deduction, and contribution limits are adjusted annually. Check the IRS for current figures rather than trusting any specific number you read once — including in this course.

contentBlocks.title: The one real exception (and it's not brackets)
contentBlocks.content: There's a narrow case where earning more *can* leave you worse off: **benefit cliffs**. Some income-tested programs — subsidies, assistance, aid — phase out or cut off at hard income thresholds, and crossing one can cost more than the raise adds. This is a real phenomenon and worth knowing if you're near such a threshold. But notice: it has nothing to do with tax brackets. Brackets are marginal and can never do this. When someone says 'the raise cost me money,' they're either wrong about brackets, or they've hit a benefit cliff — two completely different things that get blurred together constantly.

interactionSequence.prompt: Illustrative brackets: 10% up to $11k, 12% to $44k, 22% above. You earn $60,000 and get a $5,000 raise. What happens to your take-home?
options: It drops — the whole $65,000 is now taxed at 22%
It rises by $3,900 — the $5,000 is taxed at 22%, so you keep 78% of it
It rises by the full $5,000 — raises aren't taxed differently
